name: Operating System (OS)
what_it_is: The operating system is the invisible layer that runs before any app you open. Think of it like the crew backstage at a theater — you never see them, but they set up the stage, manage the lights, and make sure everything works before a single actor walks on. Without the OS, your hardware is just parts in a box. The OS is what turns it into a computer you can actually use.
analogy: Think of it like the manager of a restaurant. The manager doesn't cook food or serve customers directly — but nothing works without them. They make sure the kitchen (hardware) and the waiters (apps) can all do their jobs without stepping on each other.

examples.text: When you double-click a file, you don't do the work — the OS does. It figures out which app should open it, carves out a portion of memory for that app, and puts the window on your screen. You clicked once; the OS did a hundred things behind the scenes.

Plug in a USB drive and your OS recognizes the new device, figures out how to communicate with it, and makes the files visible in your file browser. You didn't do anything — the OS handled the entire handshake between your computer and the drive.
When two apps want to use your Wi-Fi at the same time, the OS decides how to share the network connection between them. It's constantly juggling resources so nothing crashes into anything else.

use_when: You hear someone ask "what OS are you on?" — they're asking whether you're using Windows, macOS, or Linux because the answer changes what commands you'll type and what software you can run.

definition.prompt: What is the main job of an operating system?
options: It writes your code for you
It manages your hardware and lets apps run on top of it
It connects you to the internet
It stores your files on the hard drive
definition.explanation: The OS sits between your hardware and your apps. It manages the CPU, memory, and devices — and gives every program a consistent way to use them. Without it, apps would have to talk to hardware directly, which would be chaos.

definition.prompt: Which of these is an operating system?
options: Google Chrome
Microsoft Word
macOS
VS Code
definition.explanation: macOS is Apple's operating system — the software that runs your Mac before anything else starts. Chrome, Word, and VS Code are all apps that run on top of an OS, not OS's themselves.

usage.prompt: A developer says "this app only runs on Linux." What does that mean?
options: The app only works on computers made by Linux Inc.
The app was built for the Linux OS and won't work on Windows or macOS without changes
The app requires a Linux keyboard
The app was written in the Linux programming language
usage.explanation: Programs are compiled or interpreted for a specific OS. The system calls, file formats, and available APIs differ between Windows, macOS, and Linux. An app built for Linux talks to the Linux kernel — that conversation doesn't work on a different OS.

usage.prompt: You want to install a new program. Why does the OS matter?
options: The OS controls how fast you can type
All programs work on all operating systems without differences
Different OSes use different file formats and system calls, so programs are built for specific ones
The OS determines how expensive the program will be

usage.prompt: Someone shares a `.exe` file with you. You're on a Mac. What's the problem?
options: .exe files are too large for macOS
macOS doesn't support files with more than 3 letters in the extension
.exe is a Windows executable format — macOS uses a different format and can't run it directly
You need to update your OS to open .exe files

usage.prompt: When you open two apps at the same time and your computer slows down, what is the OS doing?
options: Downloading updates in the background
Waiting for you to close one app before allocating resources
Sharing limited CPU and RAM between both apps simultaneously, causing resource contention
Restarting its internal memory manager

usage.prompt: A friend says their computer "crashed." In OS terms, what most likely happened?
options: The hard drive physically broke
The internet connection dropped
A program or the OS itself encountered an unrecoverable error and stopped executing
The computer ran out of storage space

usage.prompt: You install the same app on Windows and macOS. Why might it look slightly different on each?
options: The app has two different codebases for aesthetic reasons
Each OS provides its own UI components and rendering engine that apps use by default
macOS displays everything 20% larger than Windows
The app detects your screen resolution and adjusts accordingly

name: File System
what_it_is: The file system is how your computer keeps track of every file — where it lives, what it's called, and how to find it again. Think of it like the grid system of a city: every building has a unique address, and if you know the address, you can find it exactly. Your file system is that grid for your hard drive. Every photo, document, and code file has a specific address, and the file system is the map that tracks all of them.
analogy: It's like the filing cabinet system in an office. The cabinet itself is your hard drive. The drawers are folders. The files inside are documents. The file system is the rulebook that says how everything gets labeled and where it goes — so you can find it later.

examples.text: When you save a document, the file system records its name and exactly where it's stored on the disk. When you double-click it later, your computer doesn't scan the whole drive — it looks up the address in the file system and goes straight to it.

When you delete a file and empty the Trash, the file system removes the address from its records — but the actual data on the disk isn't immediately erased. That space is just marked as "available." Until something else is written over it, recovery tools can sometimes still find the old file.

Different OSes use different file systems: Windows uses NTFS, macOS uses APFS, and most Linux systems use ext4. When you plug a USB drive formatted in NTFS into a Mac, macOS can usually read it — but it needs to understand the NTFS rulebook to do so.

use_when: You hear someone talk about "paths," "directories," or "where a file is stored" — they're describing the file system structure. Understanding it helps you navigate the terminal confidently.

definition.prompt: What does the file system do?
options: It scans files for viruses
It organizes and tracks where every file is stored on your disk
It compresses files to save space
It syncs files to the cloud automatically
definition.explanation: The file system is the invisible layer that knows where every file lives on your disk. Without it, your hard drive would be a sea of raw data with no way to find anything. It's what makes 'open this file' actually work.

definition.prompt: Which of these best describes the file system?
options: A program you install to manage files
A type of internet storage
The rulebook your OS uses to store and find files on disk
The physical hard drive in your computer
definition.explanation: The file system is a set of rules built into the OS — not something you install, and not the physical drive itself. It defines how files get named, where they go, and how they're found. Think of it as the index for your hard drive.

usage.prompt: You save a file to your Desktop. What role does the file system play?
options: It uploads a backup to the cloud
It records the file's name and exact location on disk so it can be retrieved later
It converts the file into binary automatically
It checks if the file already exists and deletes it if so

usage.prompt: You delete a file and empty the Trash. Is the data truly gone?
options: Yes — deleting a file immediately overwrites the data
Yes — the file system erases it from the hard drive instantly
No — the file system marks the space as available but doesn't wipe it immediately
No — files are never deleted; they're just hidden
usage.explanation: Deleting a file removes its entry from the file system index — the OS no longer knows it exists. But the actual data on disk isn't wiped until something else is written to that space. That's why recovery tools can sometimes find 'deleted' files.

usage.prompt: You plug a Windows-formatted USB into a Mac. Why can the Mac sometimes read it?
options: All file systems are identical across OSes
macOS automatically reformats the drive
macOS includes support for the NTFS file system used by Windows
The Mac copies the files to a temporary location before reading them

usage.prompt: A developer mentions a "path" to a file. What are they describing?
options: The network route the file takes to download
The history of edits made to the file
The file system address that locates the file on disk
A type of encrypted file format

usage.prompt: What happens when two files have the same name in the same folder?
options: Both are kept — the file system adds a number to one
The file system allows it and merges their contents
The file system prevents it — only one file can use a given name in the same location
Both files are renamed with a timestamp

usage.prompt: Your hard drive is full. From a file system perspective, what can't happen?
options: The OS can't find existing files
The file system has no available space to record new file locations, so new files can't be saved
Existing files are automatically compressed
The file system resets to factory defaults

name: mkdir
what_it_is: `mkdir` stands for "make directory" — it creates a new folder from the terminal. Think of it like right-clicking your desktop and choosing "New Folder," except you type one command and the folder appears instantly, exactly where you want it, without touching your mouse.
analogy: Exactly like right-clicking on your desktop and choosing "New Folder" — except you can name it, place it anywhere, and even create nested folders all in one command.

examples.text: `mkdir my-project` creates a folder called `my-project` inside whichever folder you're currently in. Run `ls` afterward and it shows up in the list — the same as creating a new folder in Finder or File Explorer, just faster.

`mkdir -p src/components/buttons` creates that entire folder path in one shot, even if `src` and `components` don't exist yet. Without `-p`, you'd get an error because the parent folders are missing. The `-p` flag just means "create any missing folders along the way."
`mkdir images css js` creates three directories at once in a single command. Separating names with spaces creates each as a separate directory.

use_when: Setting up a new project structure, adding a new section to an existing project, or whenever you need to create folders from the terminal without leaving the command line.

definition.prompt: What does `mkdir` do?
options: Moves a directory to a new location
Creates a new directory
Lists the contents of a directory
Deletes a directory
definition.explanation: `mkdir` = make directory. It creates a new folder wherever you tell it to. `mv` moves things, `ls` lists things, `rm` removes things — `mkdir` is the one that makes new folders.

definition.prompt: What does `mkdir` stand for?
options: Modify Directory
Move Directory
Make Directory
Map Directory
definition.explanation: Unix commands are almost always abbreviations: `cd` = change directory, `ls` = list, `rm` = remove, `mkdir` = make directory. Once you see the pattern, you can often guess what a command does just from its name.

usage.prompt: You want to create a folder called `components` inside an existing `src` folder without navigating into it. What do you type?
options: mkdir components src
touch src/components
mkdir src/components
cd src && touch components

usage.prompt: You run `mkdir src/components/buttons` and get "No such file or directory." What flag fixes this?
options: -r
-f
-v
-p — creates parent directories as needed

usage.prompt: What command creates three folders (`css`, `js`, `images`) at once?
options: mkdir css+js+images
mkdir css; mkdir js; mkdir images
mkdir [css, js, images]
mkdir css js images

anatomy.prompt: In `mkdir -p a/b/c`, if only `a` exists, what directories get created?
options: Only `c`
Only `b/c`
`a` gets recreated, plus `b` and `c`
`b` inside `a`, and `c` inside `b` — the full path is created
anatomy.explanation: The `-p` flag creates every missing directory in the path. It also won't error if a directory already exists. So `mkdir -p a/b/c` creates `b` inside `a`, then `c` inside `b` — whatever's missing gets created.

anatomy.prompt: You run `mkdir my project` (with a space). What happens?
options: Creates a directory called `my project`
Creates nothing — spaces aren't allowed in names
Creates two directories: `my` and `project`
Creates a directory called `my_project`

build.prompt: What single command creates the directory structure `project/src/components`?
options: mkdir project src components
mkdir project && cd project && mkdir src && cd src && mkdir components
mkdir -p project/src/components
touch project/src/components

name: Processes
what_it_is: A process is what happens when a program actually runs. Every app you open — Chrome, VS Code, Spotify — the OS creates a separate process for it, giving it memory and CPU time to do its work. Think of it like a worker starting a shift: the OS sets them up with the tools they need and lets them run. When the app closes or crashes, the process ends and the OS clears everything out.
analogy: Think of a process like a chef working a shift in a kitchen. The recipe is the program (the instructions). The chef actively cooking is the process (the program in action). The kitchen manager (OS) decides how much stove time each chef gets. If a chef freezes up, the manager can remove them without shutting down the whole kitchen.

examples.text: Open Chrome and you can see multiple processes — one per tab — in Activity Monitor (Mac) or Task Manager (Windows). Each tab is isolated: if one crashes, the others keep running. That isolation is the whole point of separate processes.

When an app freezes, force-quitting it tells the OS to end that process and reclaim the memory it was using. Your other apps keep running because they're in separate processes — the frozen one was completely isolated from the rest.
When you run `node server.js`, the OS creates a process that keeps running and listening for requests. Pressing Ctrl+C sends a termination signal to that process, ending it cleanly.

use_when: You see "process exited with code 1", someone says "kill the process", or a background task is hanging. You're always dealing with a running program instance — understanding that helps you know what to kill and what to leave running.

definition.prompt: What is a process?
options: A file stored on your hard drive
A running instance of a program
A type of CPU instruction
A network connection between two computers
definition.explanation: A process is a program that's actively running — the OS has loaded it into memory and is executing it. The program file on disk is static. The process is it in action, with its own allocated memory and CPU time.

definition.prompt: What happens when a process crashes?
options: Your whole computer restarts
The OS terminates it and reclaims its memory — other processes keep running
All other processes pause until it recovers
The hard drive is checked for errors
definition.explanation: Processes are isolated from each other by the OS. When one crashes, the OS terminates it and frees its memory — but everything else continues. That isolation is by design: a buggy app shouldn't be able to take down your whole system.

usage.prompt: You see "process exited with code 1" in your terminal. What does this mean?
options: Your terminal crashed
The program ran and exited with an error
The process was killed by another user
Exit code 1 means the process succeeded
usage.explanation: Exit codes communicate how a process ended. Code 0 means success. Anything else signals an error. Code 1 is the generic failure code — you'll see it when a Node server crashes, a build script fails, or a CLI tool encounters an unhandled error.

usage.prompt: You open Chrome and Activity Monitor shows 12 Chrome processes. Why are there so many?
options: Chrome has a bug that duplicates itself
Chrome creates one process per tab for isolation and stability
Your OS is running Chrome multiple times accidentally
Chrome pre-loads processes to speed up future launches

usage.prompt: You run `node server.js` in your terminal and close the terminal window. What happens to the server process?
options: It keeps running in the background forever
It stops — closing the terminal kills the attached process
It moves to Activity Monitor and pauses
It saves its state and resumes on next launch

name: Memory (RAM)
what_it_is: RAM is your computer's short-term work surface — the space where everything currently happening lives. Think of it like a physical desk: your hard drive is the filing cabinet (large storage, but you have to get up to retrieve things), and RAM is the desk surface where you spread out everything you're actively working on. The bigger the desk, the more you can have open at once. When you close an app, its space on the desk gets cleared. When you restart, the entire desk is wiped.
analogy: Think of RAM as your desk surface. Your hard drive is the filing cabinet — it stores everything long-term. RAM is the desk — it's where you spread out what you're currently working on. The bigger your desk, the more you can have open at once. When you leave the office, you clear the desk (RAM resets), but the filing cabinet stays full.

examples.text: When you open VS Code, it loads into RAM — your files, your editor layout, your extensions all land on that "desk surface" while you work. Close VS Code and that space is immediately freed up for other apps to use.

When Node.js throws "JavaScript heap out of memory," your code filled up all the desk space it was given. Usually something kept adding to the desk without ever clearing anything off — a memory leak. The desk got so full the whole thing crashed.

When you have 20 Chrome tabs open and your computer slows down, it's because each tab consumes RAM. Closing tabs frees memory and restores performance — you're literally giving the OS more workspace to use.

use_when: You see "out of memory", "heap out of memory", or "memory leak" in an error — or when someone asks "how much RAM do you have?" They're asking about this temporary workspace your computer uses while running programs.

definition.prompt: What is RAM?
options: Permanent storage for your files and programs
Your computer's short-term workspace for actively running programs
The processor that executes your code
A type of solid-state hard drive
definition.explanation: RAM is temporary, fast memory used for everything currently running. It's not storage — when you restart, RAM is wiped completely. It's the active workspace your OS uses to run apps, hold open files, and store variables while code executes.

definition.prompt: What happens to RAM when you close an app?
options: The data is saved to your hard drive automatically
The memory is freed and becomes available for other programs
The memory stays reserved in case you reopen the app
It gets moved to the CPU cache
definition.explanation: RAM is temporary. When a process ends, the OS reclaims its memory immediately and makes it available to other processes. That's why closing unused apps can speed up your computer — you're freeing workspace for everything else.

usage.prompt: Node.js throws "JavaScript heap out of memory". What does this mean?
options: Your hard drive is full
Your code exceeded the RAM allocated to the Node.js process
Your CPU is overloaded and can't execute more code
The JavaScript file is too large to parse
usage.explanation: The heap is the portion of RAM where JavaScript stores objects and variables. "Out of memory" means your code kept allocating memory without freeing it — a memory leak. The process ran out of its RAM allocation and the OS terminated it.

usage.prompt: Your computer slows down with many browser tabs open. From a RAM perspective, why?
options: More tabs means more network requests slowing your connection
Each tab is a separate process consuming RAM — you're running low on workspace
The browser has a bug that causes slowdowns with many tabs
The OS throttles CPU speed when too many windows are open

usage.prompt: Why does restarting your computer often fix sluggish performance?
options: It installs pending OS updates that improve speed
Restarting clears all RAM, freeing memory held by crashed or slow processes
It defragments the hard drive
It resets your CPU clock speed to factory defaults

name: Directories
what_it_is: A directory is just the technical word for a folder. Your entire file system is built from directories nested inside other directories — like a set of boxes inside boxes, all the way from your outermost hard drive down to individual files. When you work in the terminal, almost every action involves navigating in and out of directories.
analogy: Like physical folders in a filing cabinet. A folder can hold documents, but it can also hold other folders. A folder called "Projects" might contain one folder per project, each of which contains the actual files. The nesting can go as deep as you need.

examples.text: Your Desktop is a directory. Inside it might be a file like `notes.txt`, plus folders like `Screenshots` or `Projects`. Inside `Projects` could be `my-app`, which contains `src`, `public`, and `node_modules` — each one its own directory, holding more files inside.

In the terminal, `ls` shows you what's in your current directory — files and folders together. Folders are often shown with a trailing `/` or in a different color depending on your terminal. It's like opening a folder in Finder, just in text form.

When you run `npm install`, it creates a `node_modules` directory inside your project containing hundreds of subdirectories — one per package. Without directory structure, managing thousands of files would be impossible.

use_when: You hear "navigate to that directory", "create a directory", or "the file is in the components directory" — it's just another word for folder. In the terminal and in code, directory is the technical term you'll see everywhere.

definition.prompt: What is a directory?
options: A type of file that stores data
A container that holds files and other directories
A shortcut that points to another location
A hidden system file created by the OS
definition.explanation: A directory is a container — it holds files and other directories. Everything on your computer exists inside a directory. The terms 'directory' and 'folder' mean the same thing; directory is the technical term used in the terminal and documentation.

definition.prompt: What does it mean for directories to be "nested"?
options: Files inside them are compressed to save space
Directories exist inside other directories, forming a hierarchy
They are protected by access permissions
They are automatically backed up to the cloud
definition.explanation: Nesting means directories inside directories. Your home directory contains a Documents directory, which contains a Projects directory, which contains your app directory. This hierarchy is how the entire file system is organized — everything has a parent.

usage.prompt: You run `ls` and see `index.html`, `style.css`, and `components`. Which one is most likely a directory?
options: index.html — it's the main file
style.css — it contains many styles
components — it has no file extension, suggesting it's a folder
All three are directories
usage.explanation: Files typically have extensions (.html, .css, .js). Directories usually don't. Seeing `components` with no extension is a strong signal it's a directory containing component files — exactly the kind of structure you'd see in a web project.

usage.prompt: A developer says "go into the src directory". What are they asking you to do?
options: Open the src file in a text editor
Use `cd src` to navigate into the src folder in your terminal
Search for all files named src
Delete everything outside the src directory

usage.prompt: You delete a directory. What happens to the files inside it?
options: They move to the Desktop automatically
They are deleted along with the directory
They become root-level files
They move to the Trash separately from the directory

name: Dotfiles
what_it_is: Dotfiles are files or folders whose names start with a dot (`.`). On Mac and Linux, anything starting with a dot is hidden by default — invisible in Finder and not shown by `ls` unless you specifically ask. That's by design: dotfiles are almost always configuration files that tools read quietly in the background. They're not meant to clutter your everyday view.
analogy: Like the settings panel behind a restaurant's front counter — customers don't see it, but everything depends on it. The WiFi password, the POS system preferences, the staff rota — all hidden from view but critical to how the place runs. Dotfiles are your dev tools' hidden settings.

examples.text: `.env` stores secrets — API keys, database passwords, environment-specific settings. It's hidden so it doesn't accidentally show up when you're screensharing, and it must always be in `.gitignore` so it's never committed to your repository.

`.gitignore` tells Git which files to completely ignore — things like `node_modules` or `.env`. It starts with a dot to stay out of your way in normal file browsing, but Git reads it automatically every time you commit.

`.eslintrc`, `.prettierrc`, `.babelrc` are all dotfiles that configure developer tools. When you install ESLint, it reads `.eslintrc` for your rule preferences. AI and install scripts usually create these — you rarely write them from scratch.

use_when: When you see a file starting with `.` in your project — especially `.env`, `.gitignore`, or any `.*rc` file — that's a dotfile. It's configuration. Don't delete it blindly, never commit `.env` to GitHub, and use `ls -a` to see them all.

definition.prompt: What makes a file a dotfile?
options: It ends with a `.txt` extension
Its name starts with a dot (.)
It is stored in the system directory
It requires special software to open
definition.explanation: Any file or directory whose name starts with a dot is a dotfile. The leading dot signals 'this is configuration' and also triggers the OS to hide it from normal directory listings. It's a Unix convention that every developer encounters constantly.

definition.prompt: Why are dotfiles hidden by default on Mac and Linux?
options: They contain encrypted data that must stay secure
To keep directories tidy — config files don't need to be browsed regularly
They are system files that cannot be modified by users
They are too large to display in normal file listings
definition.explanation: Hiding dotfiles is a convention for tidiness, not security. It keeps your home directory and project folders clean — you see your actual work files, not the dozens of config files every tool creates. Use `ls -a` to reveal them all.

usage.prompt: You see a `.env` file in a project. What should you do with it?
options: Delete it — it's a hidden system file you don't need
Commit it to GitHub so your teammates have the values
Keep it local, add it to `.gitignore`, and never commit it
Rename it to remove the dot so it's visible in Finder
usage.explanation: .env files contain API keys, database passwords, and secrets. Committing them to GitHub exposes those secrets — even in private repos, leaks happen. Always add .env to .gitignore. Share the variable names with teammates but never the actual values.

usage.prompt: You run `ls` in your project folder and can't see `.gitignore` even though it exists. How do you reveal it?
options: The file was deleted — recreate it
Run `ls -a` — the `-a` flag shows hidden dotfiles
.gitignore is stored in a different location by Git
Your terminal doesn't have permission to see it

usage.prompt: A teammate says "check your rc files". What are they referring to?
options: Files with a `.rc` extension in your Documents folder
Dotfiles ending in `rc` — config files for tools like ESLint, Babel, and Prettier
Recently closed files in your code editor
Runtime config files stored in `/etc/`

name: File Permissions
what_it_is: Every file on Mac and Linux has a set of rules attached to it controlling who can read it, write to it, or run it as a program. These are called permissions, and they apply to three groups: the file's owner, a defined group of users, and everyone else. You'll rarely think about them until something breaks — but when you see "permission denied," permissions are the reason.
analogy: Like access levels in a building. Some rooms anyone can enter (read), some rooms only staff can modify (write), and some doors only security can unlock (execute). The building manager — root — can go anywhere. Your badge (your user account) determines your access.

examples.text: If you try to run a script and get "permission denied," the file isn't marked as executable — the OS won't run it as a program. `chmod +x script.sh` adds that permission. Think of it as the OS requiring a specific stamp before a file is allowed to execute.

Your `.env` file should be readable only by you. `chmod 600 .env` sets it to owner read/write only — no one else on the system can open it. On a shared server, this is standard practice to keep your secrets private.

When you run `sudo` before a command, you're temporarily acting as the root user — the superuser who has permission to do anything on the system. That's why `sudo npm install -g` works when `npm install -g` gives you a permission error.

use_when: You get a "permission denied" error, need to run a shell script, configure server files, or notice that a command works with `sudo` but not without it — permissions are involved every time.

definition.prompt: What do file permissions control?
options: The file's size and type on disk
Who can read, write to, or execute a file
Where a file is physically stored on disk
How fast a file can be accessed by the CPU
definition.explanation: Permissions are a security layer on every file and directory. They define three actions (read, write, execute) for three groups (owner, group, others). When the OS says 'permission denied', it means your user account doesn't have the required permission for that action.

usage.prompt: You get "permission denied" when trying to run a shell script. What's the most likely fix?
options: Delete and recreate the file
Run `chmod +x script.sh` to add execute permission
Move the file to a different directory
Open it in a text editor before running
usage.explanation: A shell script is just a text file until you mark it executable with chmod +x. Without the execute bit, the OS refuses to run it as a program. chmod +x means 'add execute permission' (+x) to this file for the owner.

usage.prompt: Why does adding `sudo` before a command sometimes fix "permission denied" errors?
options: `sudo` bypasses all security checks permanently
`sudo` runs the command as root — the superuser with permission to do anything
`sudo` installs any missing packages before running
`sudo` resets the file's permissions before executing
usage.explanation: Root is the superuser account that can read, write, and execute anything on the system. `sudo` (superuser do) runs a single command as root. That's why it fixes permission errors — root doesn't get denied access to anything.

usage.prompt: What does `chmod 600 .env` do?
options: Makes `.env` readable by everyone on the system
Sets `.env` to owner read/write only — no other user can read it
Deletes `.env` from the file system
Makes `.env` executable as a script

usage.prompt: A file is set to read-only for the owner, no permissions for anyone else. A different user tries to open it. What happens?
options: The file opens normally for all users
They get a permission denied error
The OS prompts them for the owner's password
The file is automatically copied to their home directory

name: Absolute Paths
what_it_is: An absolute path is the full, exact address of a file or folder — starting from the very root of the file system and going all the way to the destination. No matter where you currently are when you type it, it always points to the same place. On Mac and Linux, absolute paths start with `/`. On Windows, they start with a drive letter like `C:\`.
analogy: Like a full mailing address. "123 Main Street, Springfield, IL 62701" works no matter where you're sending from — New York or next door. An absolute path is the same: `/Users/alex/projects/my-app/index.js` points to exactly one file on the entire system, from anywhere.

examples.text: `/Users/alex/Desktop/notes.txt` is an absolute path on Mac. No matter which folder your terminal is in, this path always refers to that exact file. You can open it, copy it, or delete it from anywhere — the path tells the whole story.

Error messages often include absolute paths: `Cannot find module '/home/user/app/config.js'`. The system looked for that exact file at that exact location and didn't find it. The error is already telling you exactly where to go looking.

Environment variables often store absolute paths. `PATH=/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin` tells the OS exactly where to look for executable programs — each entry is an absolute path to a directory of commands.

use_when: Use absolute paths in scripts, config files, and environment variables — anywhere the path needs to work regardless of where the command is run from. If a path will be used in different contexts or by different people, absolute is safer.

definition.prompt: What is an absolute path?
options: A path relative to your current directory
The full address of a file starting from the root of the file system
A shortened alias for a frequently used file location
A path that only works on one operating system
definition.explanation: An absolute path is complete and self-contained. It starts from the root (/ on Mac/Linux, C:\ on Windows) and spells out every step to the file. It works regardless of where you currently are in the file system — no context needed.

definition.prompt: Which of these is an absolute path on Mac?
options: ../components/Button.js
./src/index.js
/Users/alex/projects/app/index.js
node_modules/express/index.js
definition.explanation: /Users/alex/projects/app/index.js starts from the root (/) and gives the complete location. The others are relative paths (./ and ../) or package-relative paths. An absolute path on Mac/Linux always starts with /.

usage.prompt: You write a deploy script that references `/Users/alex/data.csv`. Your teammate runs it on their machine. What happens?
options: It works fine — absolute paths always work
It fails — their username isn't alex so that path doesn't exist on their machine
The file is copied to their machine automatically
The script asks them to locate the file manually
usage.explanation: Absolute paths that include your username or home directory structure only work on your machine. Your teammate's home directory is /Users/theirname — the path /Users/alex/ doesn't exist for them. Use environment variables or relative paths in scripts meant to run on multiple machines.

usage.prompt: An error says "Cannot find module '/home/user/app/config.js'". What does this tell you?
options: The module has a syntax error
No file exists at that absolute path — it was moved, deleted, or the path is wrong
You need to install the module with npm
The file has incorrect permissions

usage.prompt: On Windows, which of these is an absolute path?
options: ./src/app.js
../config/settings.json
C:\Users\Alex\Documents\project\index.js
node_modules/lodash/index.js

name: Relative Paths
what_it_is: A relative path gives directions starting from where you currently are — not from the root of the file system. `./` means "right here," and `../` means "go up one level." They're the paths you use in import statements in code, and they're the paths that break if a file gets moved somewhere else. Shorter to type, but they depend on context.
analogy: Like giving directions from where you're standing. "Turn left, go two blocks, it's the red building" only works if you're at the same starting point. Give those same directions to someone on a different street and they end up somewhere wrong. Relative paths work exactly this way — they depend entirely on where you are.

examples.text: In a React component, `import Button from './components/Button'` is a relative path — it means "look for `components/Button` starting from this file's current folder." Move that file to a different folder and the import breaks immediately, because the starting point changed.

`../../config.json` means "go up two levels, then find config.json." If you're in `/projects/app/src/utils/`, that resolves to `/projects/app/config.json`. Count the `../` segments to count how many levels up you're going.

When the terminal says "no such file or directory" even though the file exists, you're often using a relative path while in the wrong working directory. `./script.sh` looks for the script in your current location — not wherever it actually lives.

use_when: Relative paths are what you write in import statements and `require()` calls. They're also what trips you up in the terminal when you're not in the right directory. Whenever you see `./` or `../` — that's a relative path.

definition.prompt: What is a relative path?
options: A path that starts from the root of the file system
A path that describes a file's location starting from your current position
A path that uses the file's full absolute address
A shortened URL for accessing web files
definition.explanation: Relative paths start from where you are, not from the root. ./ means 'here', ../ means 'one level up'. They're shorter to write but context-dependent — the same relative path means different things depending on where you are in the file system.

definition.prompt: What does `../` mean in a path?
options: Go into a subdirectory one level down
Go up one level to the parent directory
Go all the way to the root directory
Go to the home directory
definition.explanation: ../ means 'parent directory' — one level up. ../../ means two levels up. If you're in /projects/app/src/ and reference ../../, you end up at /projects/. Count the ../ segments to count how many directory levels you're climbing.

usage.prompt: You move a React component to a different folder and its imports break. Why?
options: React doesn't support moving files
The relative paths in the imports now point to wrong locations because the file moved
The file's permissions changed when it was moved
You need to reinstall node_modules after moving files
usage.explanation: Relative paths are calculated from the file's current location. When you move the file, its relative paths now start from a different place. ./components/Button now looks for Button relative to the new location — which may be wrong. That's why IDEs offer to auto-update imports when you move files.

usage.prompt: You're in `/projects/app/src/`. An import says `../../config`. Where does it point?
options: /projects/app/src/config
/projects/app/config
/projects/config
/config

usage.prompt: Why do relative paths work better than absolute paths in import statements?
options: Relative paths are faster for the OS to resolve
Relative paths work on any machine regardless of the project's install location
Absolute paths don't work in JavaScript
Relative paths are required by the Node.js specification

name: Working Directory
what_it_is: Your working directory is simply the folder your terminal is currently inside. Every command you run is relative to it — `ls` shows its contents, new files land there, and relative paths start from there. Think of it as the "You Are Here" dot on a map. It's always somewhere, and it changes when you navigate.
analogy: Like your physical desk in an office. When you say "the file is right here", you mean on your desk. The working directory is where "here" is in your terminal. Move to a different desk (`cd`) and "here" changes completely.

examples.text: When you open a terminal, you start in your home directory. Type `pwd` to see the full path of where you are. Type `ls` to see what's inside. Those two commands together orient you immediately.

"Run this from your project root" means: before running the command, make sure your terminal is inside the top-level folder of your project. Run `npm install` from the wrong folder and `node_modules` ends up in the wrong place.

When you see "no such file or directory" for a file that definitely exists, check `pwd` first. You're probably not where you think you are — the file exists, but not relative to your current working directory.

use_when: Every time a terminal command fails with "file not found" — check your working directory first with `pwd`. Most of the time, you're just not in the directory you think you're in.

definition.prompt: What is the working directory?
options: The directory where your OS is installed
The directory your terminal is currently inside — where all relative paths start from
The most recently visited directory in your terminal history
The directory containing your project's main entry file
definition.explanation: Your working directory is your current location in the terminal. Every relative path starts here, every file you create lands here, and every command that looks for a file starts searching here. `pwd` (print working directory) shows you exactly where you are.

definition.prompt: How do you check your current working directory in the terminal?
options: ls
cd
pwd
dir
definition.explanation: pwd stands for 'print working directory'. It outputs the absolute path of your current location. It's the fastest way to orient yourself when something isn't working — before debugging a path error, always confirm where you actually are.

usage.prompt: You run `npm install` and `node_modules` appears in the wrong folder. What most likely went wrong?
options: npm has a bug that sometimes installs in the wrong location
Your working directory was wrong — you ran the command from the wrong folder
You need to specify the install location with a flag
`node_modules` always goes in your home directory
usage.explanation: npm install creates node_modules in your current working directory. If you ran it from a parent folder or the wrong project, that's where it goes. Always confirm you're in your project root with pwd before running install commands.

usage.prompt: A developer says "run this command from the project root". What does that mean?
options: Run it as the root/administrator user
Navigate to your project's top-level directory first, then run the command
Run it from the terminal application's own installation folder
It doesn't matter where you run it from

name: Home Directory (~)
what_it_is: The home directory is the personal folder the OS creates for your user account. It's where your Documents, Desktop, Downloads, and personal config files live. In the terminal, `~` is shorthand for it — the shell automatically expands it to your full home directory path, whatever it happens to be on your machine.
analogy: Like your personal office in a large building. Everyone has their own office with their own things. When you open a terminal, you start there. You can go anywhere in the building (navigate to other directories), but `cd ~` always brings you back to your office.

examples.text: Open a new terminal and you start in your home directory automatically. Run `pwd` and it shows something like `/Users/yourname`. Run `ls` and you'll see Desktop, Documents, Downloads, and any hidden dotfiles sitting there.

`~/projects/my-app` expands to `/Users/yourname/projects/my-app`. Using `~` is a shortcut that always works regardless of your actual username — you don't have to hardcode `/Users/alex/` into anything.
Running `cd ~` from anywhere in the terminal immediately returns you to your home directory. It's the fastest way to reset your location when you've navigated somewhere deep.

use_when: When you see `~` in a path or command — `cd ~`, `~/.ssh/`, `~/projects` — it means "starting from my home directory". It's a shorthand that works for any user on any machine.

definition.prompt: What does `~` represent in a terminal path?
options: The root directory of the entire file system
Your user account's home directory
Your current working directory
The previous directory you were in
definition.explanation: ~ is a shorthand the shell expands to your home directory's full path. On Mac it becomes /Users/yourname, on Linux /home/yourname. It's a convenience — instead of typing your full home path, you type ~ and the shell substitutes the real path automatically.

definition.prompt: What does `cd ~` do?
options: Takes you to the root directory (/)
Takes you to your home directory from wherever you are
Takes you to the last directory you visited
Takes you up exactly one directory level
definition.explanation: cd ~ navigates to your home directory from anywhere in the terminal. No matter how deep you've navigated into a file system, cd ~ resets you back to your starting point. It's the quickest way to reorient yourself.

usage.prompt: The path `~/projects/app` is equivalent to what on a Mac for user "alex"?
options: /projects/app (starting from root)
/Users/alex/projects/app (starting from home directory)
./projects/app (starting from current directory)
The nearest projects folder the shell can find
usage.explanation: ~ expands to your full home directory path. ~/projects/app becomes /Users/alex/projects/app on Mac. It's a portable shorthand — the shell substitutes each user's actual home path, so it works correctly for any user.

usage.prompt: A script references `~/.ssh/id_rsa`. What is it pointing to?
options: A system-wide SSH key in the OS installation directory
An SSH private key file inside a `.ssh` folder in your home directory
A temporary file created during SSH connections
A network configuration file on a remote server

name: Terminal & Shell
what_it_is: The terminal is the window you type commands into. The shell is the program running inside that window that actually understands what you type and makes things happen. Most people use these words interchangeably, and that's fine — but they're technically different things. The terminal is the interface (the window); the shell is the interpreter (the brain). Common shells are bash and zsh.
analogy: Think of it like a phone call. The terminal is the phone — the device you hold and speak into. The shell is the operator on the other end who understands what you say and routes it to the right place. The terminal just captures your input; the shell figures out what it means and does something with it.

examples.text: You open Terminal on your Mac. That app is the terminal — it's just a window. Inside it, zsh is running — that's the shell. When you type `ls`, the terminal shows it on screen, but zsh is the one that looks up what `ls` means, runs it, and prints the output back to you.

iTerm2, Terminal.app, and Windows Terminal are all different terminal apps — different windows. But they can all run the same shell (bash, zsh, PowerShell). Changing terminal apps is like getting a new desk. Changing shells is like changing the language you work in.

When a tutorial says 'open a terminal and run this command' — they mean: open a terminal app, and the shell inside it will process the command. The distinction matters when something fails, because the error might come from the shell's rules, not from the terminal app.

use_when: Any time you're told to 'run a command' or 'open a terminal' — you need to know you're launching a shell inside a terminal window. If something doesn't work, knowing whether the problem is the terminal (display issue) or the shell (interpretation issue) helps you debug it.

definition.prompt: What is the difference between a terminal and a shell?
options: They are the same thing — just different words for the same program
The terminal is the window you type in; the shell is the program that interprets your commands
The terminal runs on Linux; the shell runs on macOS
The terminal stores your files; the shell connects to the internet
definition.explanation: The terminal is the interface — the window. The shell is the program inside it that reads your commands and executes them. You can run different shells inside the same terminal app, and different terminal apps can run the same shell.

definition.prompt: Which of these is a shell?
options: Terminal.app
iTerm2
Windows Terminal
bash
definition.explanation: bash (Bourne Again Shell) is a shell — the program that interprets commands. Terminal.app, iTerm2, and Windows Terminal are terminal emulators — they're the windows you open to access a shell.

usage.prompt: A developer tells you 'this script only works in bash, not zsh.' What does that mean for you?
options: You need to download a different terminal app
You need to switch which shell is running — type bash first or change your default shell
The script only works on Linux computers
You need to install a new version of macOS
usage.explanation: bash and zsh are different shells. You can switch shells by typing `bash` in your terminal — that starts a bash session. The terminal app doesn't change, just the shell running inside it.

usage.prompt: You open a terminal and type a command. Nothing happens and there's no error. What is the most likely explanation?
options: The terminal app crashed
The shell didn't receive your input
The command ran and produced no output (that's normal for some commands)
Your keyboard is broken
usage.explanation: Many shell commands produce no output on success — `mkdir`, `cd`, `touch` all do their job silently. No output doesn't mean nothing happened. If there was an error, the shell would have printed one.

usage.prompt: What is zsh?
options: A terminal application made by Apple
A type of file compression format
A shell — an alternative to bash that is now the default on macOS
A command for listing files on Linux
usage.explanation: zsh (Z shell) is a shell — like bash, but with more features and customization options. Apple switched the macOS default shell from bash to zsh in 2019. Both zsh and bash understand the same basic commands.

name: Commands, Flags & Arguments
what_it_is: A command tells the shell what to do. Flags (also called options) are switches that change how the command behaves — they usually start with a dash (`-`) for short flags or two dashes (`--`) for long ones. Arguments are the inputs you're passing to the command, like a filename or a path. Most commands follow this pattern: `command [flags] [arguments]`.
analogy: Think of ordering coffee. The command is 'coffee' — what you want. Flags are modifiers: 'large', 'iced', 'decaf' — they change how it's made. The argument is the specific thing it applies to: 'oat milk latte.' So `ls -la /home` is like saying 'list, long format, show all, for the /home directory.'

examples.text: `git commit -m "my message"` — `git` is the program, `commit` is the action, `-m` is a flag meaning "a message follows," and `"my message"` is that message. Each part has a specific role.
`ls -la` — `ls` lists files, `-l` means "show details," and `-a` means "include hidden files." Combining them as `-la` gives you both at once. Writing `ls -l -a` is identical — just more verbose.

`grep --ignore-case 'error' app.log` — grep is the command, --ignore-case is a long flag (two dashes, full word), 'error' is the pattern argument, and app.log is the file argument. Long flags are more readable in scripts.

use_when: Reading docs or copy-pasting commands from tutorials — knowing the pattern lets you decode any command even if you've never used it before. When a flag isn't doing what you expect, this is where to look first.

definition.prompt: In the command `npm install --save-dev jest`, what is `--save-dev`?
options: The command being run
The package being installed
A flag that modifies how npm install behaves
An error message
definition.explanation: `--save-dev` is a long flag (two dashes + full word). It tells npm to install jest as a dev dependency rather than a regular dependency. Flags modify the behavior of the command — without it, jest would be installed differently.

definition.prompt: What does a double dash (--) before a word usually indicate in a shell command?
options: A comment — the shell ignores everything after it
A long-form flag or option
A required argument that must always be provided
The end of the command
definition.explanation: Double dashes prefix long-form flags like `--verbose`, `--output`, `--help`. Single dashes prefix short flags like `-v`, `-o`, `-h`. Long flags are more readable in scripts; short flags save typing interactively.

usage.prompt: You run a command and it doesn't do quite what you wanted. Where do you look first to understand what options are available?
options: Restart your computer
Run `command --help` or `man command` to see available flags and arguments
Open the terminal settings menu
Check your internet connection
usage.explanation: `--help` is a near-universal flag that prints usage information. `man command` (manual) gives the full documentation. These are the standard ways to discover what flags and arguments a command accepts.

usage.prompt: What is the argument in the command `cat README.md`?
options: cat
README
README.md
There is no argument — only a flag
usage.explanation: `cat` is the command, `README.md` is the argument — the file you want cat to read and print. Arguments are the inputs the command operates on. Flags modify behavior; arguments provide the data.

usage.prompt: You see the command `git log --oneline -10`. What does -10 likely do?
options: Deletes the last 10 commits
Limits output to the last 10 log entries
Shows logs from 10 days ago
Increases font size in the terminal
usage.explanation: Numeric flags are often used to limit output count. `git log -10` shows only the last 10 commits. When you see a number as a short flag, it usually means 'how many' — limit the output.

name: Sudo
what_it_is: Some commands need administrator-level access to work — installing system-wide software, editing protected config files, managing services. `sudo` ("superuser do") lets you run a single command with that elevated access. Think of it as a master key that unlocks the restricted area, but only for that one operation. It asks for your password first to confirm it's really you.
analogy: Think of it like a keycard system. Most staff have access to the regular areas. But some doors — server rooms, admin offices — require a manager-level keycard. Sudo is like borrowing that keycard for a single action. You swipe it, do the thing, and the elevated access goes away immediately after. You're not becoming a manager permanently — just unlocking one door.

examples.text: `sudo apt install nginx` — without `sudo`, the package manager refuses to install system-wide software because it requires root access. Adding `sudo` in front grants it just for this one command.
`sudo nano /etc/hosts` — the hosts file controls how your machine resolves domain names. It's protected from normal users to prevent accidental changes. `sudo` lets you open and edit it.

After running `sudo`, subsequent sudo commands within a few minutes won't re-ask for your password. The elevated access has a short timeout (typically 5–15 minutes) before you'd need to authenticate again.

use_when: A command fails with 'permission denied' — try prefixing it with sudo. Also when following installation guides that require writing to system directories, managing services with systemctl, or editing files outside your home directory.

definition.prompt: What does `sudo` do when placed before a command?
options: It runs the command faster by using a dedicated processor
It runs the command with administrator (root) privileges
It runs the command in a separate window
It previews the command without executing it
definition.explanation: sudo grants root (superuser) privileges for that one command. Without sudo, your user account doesn't have permission to touch protected files or system resources. With sudo, the shell temporarily elevates your privileges, executes the command, then returns to normal.

definition.prompt: You run a command and see: `Permission denied`. What should you try first?
options: Reinstall the operating system
Add `sudo` before the command
Change your username
Restart the terminal
definition.explanation: Permission denied means your current user account doesn't have access to that file or operation. Adding sudo runs the command as root, which bypasses most permission restrictions. This is the standard first step when a command is blocked by permissions.

usage.prompt: Why is it risky to run everything with sudo by default?
options: It makes commands run slower
Root access can delete or overwrite critical system files if you make a mistake
It logs out other users
It permanently removes file permissions from your account
usage.explanation: Root has no guardrails — it can overwrite or delete anything on the system. Running a broken script with sudo can damage the OS itself. The principle of least privilege says you should only use elevated access when necessary, not as a habit.

usage.prompt: A tutorial says 'run this as root.' What is the easiest way to do this on Linux/macOS?
options: Create a new user account called 'root'
Prefix the command with `sudo`
Log out and log back in as administrator
Add `--root` flag to the command
usage.explanation: Prefixing with sudo is the standard way to run a command with root privileges without switching user accounts. You stay logged in as yourself, and sudo grants temporary elevated access for that one command.

usage.prompt: After typing `sudo` and entering your password, how long does it stay active before asking again?
options: It never expires — you only enter it once per session
5–15 minutes by default — subsequent sudo commands within that window won't re-prompt
Exactly 60 seconds
It expires after one command — every sudo prompts for password
usage.explanation: sudo caches your credentials for a short window (typically 5–15 minutes). During that period, sudo commands won't ask for your password again. After the timeout, it prompts again. This balances convenience and security.

name: Your Current Location (pwd)
what_it_is: `pwd` (print working directory) tells you exactly where you are in the file system right now. No arguments needed — just type `pwd`, hit enter, and the shell prints the full path of your current location. It's the terminal equivalent of "You Are Here."
analogy: Like pressing the 'You Are Here' button on a mall map. You don't move anywhere — you just get told your current location as a full path, so you can orient yourself.

examples.text: You've been `cd`-ing around and lost track of where you landed. Run `pwd` and it prints something like `/Users/alex/projects/myapp/src/components` — you immediately know your exact position without having to backtrack.
Inside a shell script: `current_dir=$(pwd)` captures your current location into a variable. You can then `cd` somewhere else to do work, and reference `$current_dir` to come back when you're done.
On a remote server you just SSH'd into: the first thing you run is `pwd` to confirm you're in the expected directory before running any commands that modify files.

use_when: Whenever you've changed directories several times and lost track of where you are. Also useful in scripts when you need to capture the starting directory, or when checking your location before running destructive commands.

definition.prompt: What does the pwd command do?
options: Changes your current directory to the home folder
Prints the full path of the directory you're currently in
Lists the files in the current directory
Shows your username and system info
definition.explanation: pwd stands for 'print working directory.' It outputs the absolute path of where you currently are in the file system. No arguments needed — just type pwd and hit enter.

usage.prompt: You ran `cd` several times in a row. You're not sure where you are. What command tells you your current location?
options: ls
cd
pwd
whoami
usage.explanation: pwd (print working directory) tells you exactly where you are in the file system. It's the go-to command when you've navigated around and need to re-orient.

usage.prompt: Inside a shell script, a developer writes: `start_dir=$(pwd)`. What is this doing?
options: Running pwd to print the directory to the screen
Storing the current directory path in a variable called start_dir
Changing the current directory to a variable
Checking if the directory exists
usage.explanation: $(...) runs a command and captures its output as a value. So `start_dir=$(pwd)` stores the current directory path in the variable start_dir. This is commonly used in scripts to remember where you started before navigating elsewhere.

anatomy.prompt: pwd takes no arguments. Why not?
options: It's a broken command from early Unix that was never updated
It reports the current directory, which is already known — there's nothing to specify
It only works on certain operating systems where arguments are disabled
Arguments are optional — you can pass a directory to check its path
anatomy.explanation: pwd tells you where you currently are — that's a fact the shell already knows. There's no input to provide. It's a pure read operation: ask the shell for its current location, and it prints the answer.

build.prompt: You're writing a script that needs to remember the starting directory so it can return to it later. Which line correctly captures it?
options: `start=$(ls)`
`start_dir=$(pwd)`
`pwd start_dir`
`set start_dir pwd`
build.explanation: `start_dir=$(pwd)` runs pwd and captures its output (the current path) into the variable start_dir. Later in the script, you can run `cd "$start_dir"` to return to where you started.

name: ls
what_it_is: `ls` lists the contents of a directory — all the files and folders inside it. With no arguments, it shows your current directory. Pass it a path and it shows that directory instead. The most useful flags: `-l` (long format — shows size, date, and permissions), `-a` (includes hidden dotfiles), and `-la` (both together).
analogy: Like opening a drawer and looking inside — ls shows you what's there. -l adds labels to each item (size, date, type). -a shows the secret compartment in the back of the drawer that normally isn't visible.

examples.text: `ls -la` — the most commonly used version. Shows everything in the current directory in detail, including hidden files like `.env` and `.gitignore`. If you want to see every file that's actually here, this is the command.
`ls src/components` — shows what's inside a specific folder without having to navigate into it. A quick way to check a folder's contents before you `cd` there.
`ls -lh` — the -h flag makes file sizes human-readable (KB, MB, GB instead of raw bytes). Useful when you want to know how large files are without needing a calculator.

example_command:
ls -la
# Output:
# drwxr-xr-x  8 alex staff  256 Mar 20 09:12 .
# -rw-r--r--  1 alex staff 1.2K Mar 19 14:30 README.md
# -rw-------  1 alex staff  512 Mar 18 10:00 .env
use_when: Any time you want to see what's in a directory — before cd-ing in, after creating files, or when you suspect something isn't where it should be. Also useful for spotting hidden files that won't show in a file manager.

definition.prompt: What does `ls -a` do differently from plain `ls`?
options: Lists files alphabetically instead of by date
Shows hidden files (those starting with a dot) that ls normally hides
Lists files in all subdirectories recursively
Shows file sizes in human-readable format
definition.explanation: Files and directories starting with a dot (like .env, .git, .gitignore) are hidden by default. Plain ls doesn't show them. The -a flag includes them in the output. This is important when you're looking for config files or checking if .git exists.

usage.prompt: You just created a new .env file in your project. You run `ls` and don't see it. What should you run instead?
options: `ls --show-all`
`ls -a`
`ls -l`
`ls hidden`
usage.explanation: Files starting with a dot are hidden from plain ls. The -a flag reveals them. `ls -a` or `ls -la` will show .env, .gitignore, .git, and other dotfiles that are otherwise invisible.

usage.prompt: What does the -l flag add to ls output?
options: It limits results to the last 10 files
It shows details: permissions, owner, file size, and modification date
It sorts by file size instead of name
It lists only directories, not files
usage.explanation: The -l flag switches to long format. Each line shows file permissions, owner, group, size (in bytes by default), modification date, and filename. This is useful when you need to check who owns a file or when it was last changed.

usage.prompt: You want to see the contents of the `src/` directory without navigating into it. What do you type?
options: `cd src/ && ls`
`ls src/`
`open src/`
`dir src/`
usage.explanation: You can pass a path as an argument to ls — it lists that directory without you having to cd into it. This is faster when you just want to quickly check what's inside a folder.

anatomy.prompt: In the command `ls -la /home/alex`, what does `/home/alex` represent?
options: A flag that enables admin mode
The directory to list — ls will show its contents instead of the current directory
A file to display information about
The output destination for the listing
anatomy.explanation: When you pass a path to ls, it lists that directory's contents instead of the current one. `/home/alex` is the argument — the directory you want to inspect. Without it, ls would list wherever you currently are.

name: cd
what_it_is: `cd` (change directory) moves you to a different folder. You pass it a path — relative or absolute. A few shortcuts worth memorizing: `cd ..` goes up one level, `cd ~` takes you home, and `cd -` takes you back to wherever you just were.
analogy: Like walking through hallways in a building. cd is the act of moving — you tell it where to go and you end up there. `cd ..` is taking one step back toward the exit. `cd ~` is taking the elevator directly to your office. `cd -` is the 'back' button.

examples.text: `cd projects/myapp` — moves you into a subfolder using a relative path from where you currently are. You don't need the full address as long as you're already in the right parent folder.
`cd ..` — moves you up one level. If you're in `/Users/alex/projects/myapp`, running `cd ..` takes you to `/Users/alex/projects`. It's the terminal equivalent of clicking the back arrow in Finder.
`cd -` — takes you back to the previous directory. If you jumped from `/projects` to `/var/log` to check something, `cd -` brings you right back to `/projects` without retyping the path.

example_command:
cd /Users/alex/projects/myapp
# No output on success — run pwd to confirm
use_when: Any time you need to navigate to a directory to run commands from it — starting a project, navigating to a log directory, or getting into the right folder before running npm install or git commands.

definition.prompt: What does `cd ..` do?
options: Navigates to the home directory
Goes up one directory level to the parent directory
Returns to the previous directory you were in
Creates a new directory called '..'
definition.explanation: `..` always refers to the parent directory. So `cd ..` moves you one level up. If you're in `/Users/alex/projects/myapp`, `cd ..` takes you to `/Users/alex/projects`.

usage.prompt: You navigated deep into a folder structure and now want to return to your home directory quickly. What's the shortest command?
options: `cd /home`
`cd ~`
`cd root`
usage.explanation: `cd ~` takes you directly to your home directory from anywhere. ~ is a shell shortcut that always expands to your home path — no matter how deep in the file system you are.

usage.prompt: What does `cd -` do?
options: Deletes the current directory
Goes up one directory level
Navigates to the previous directory you were in
Shows a list of recently visited directories
usage.explanation: `cd -` toggles between the current directory and the last one you were in. It's like a 'back' button. Useful when you need to jump to a different part of the file system briefly and then return.

usage.prompt: cd produces no output when it succeeds. How do you confirm you moved to the right place?
options: Look for a success message in the output
Run `pwd` to print the current directory
Run `cd` again to verify
Check the terminal title bar
usage.explanation: cd is silent on success — no output means it worked. Run `pwd` immediately after to confirm your current directory. Many terminals also show the current path in the prompt, which updates automatically.

anatomy.prompt: In `cd /Users/alex/projects`, what kind of path is `/Users/alex/projects`?
options: A relative path — it starts from where you currently are
An absolute path — it starts from the root of the file system
A shortcut — it's a built-in alias
A flag — it modifies cd's behavior
anatomy.explanation: Paths starting with `/` are absolute — they specify the full location from the root of the file system. Paths without a leading `/` are relative — they're interpreted from your current directory. cd works with both.

build.prompt: You're in `/Users/alex` and want to navigate to `/Users/alex/projects/myapp/src`. Which command gets you there most directly?
options: `cd projects myapp src`
`cd projects/myapp/src`
`cd /projects/myapp/src`
`cd ~/projects/myapp/src`
build.explanation: `cd projects/myapp/src` uses a relative path — starting from your current location `/Users/alex` and navigating into `projects/myapp/src`. You can also use the full absolute path or `~/projects/myapp/src`, but the relative path is shortest when you're already in the right starting directory.

name: cp & mv
what_it_is: `cp` copies a file or folder. `mv` moves it — or renames it, since moving something to the same folder with a different name is just a rename. Both take a source and a destination as arguments. To copy a whole folder and everything inside it, use `cp -r` (recursive). `mv` doesn't need that flag.
analogy: cp is like a photocopier — the original stays put, and you get a copy. mv is like picking something up off the table and putting it somewhere else — the original is gone from its old location. To rename something is just moving it to the same place with a different name.

examples.text: `cp config.example .env` — makes a copy of the example config file as a new `.env` file. The original stays, you get a fresh copy. This is a common first step when setting up a new project.
`mv old-name.js new-name.js` — renames the file. That's it. `mv` to the same location with a different name is just a rename — there's no separate rename command in the shell.
`cp -r src/ src-backup/` — copies the entire src directory and everything inside it into src-backup/. The -r flag means recursive — without it, cp would refuse to copy a directory.

example_command:
cp file.txt backup.txt
mv old-name.txt new-name.txt
use_when: Backing up files before editing, reorganizing a project's folder structure, setting up config files from examples, or renaming files without leaving the terminal.

definition.prompt: What is the key difference between cp and mv?
options: cp works on files; mv only works on directories
cp leaves the original in place; mv removes the original from its old location
cp requires sudo; mv does not
cp is faster than mv for large files
definition.explanation: cp copies — both the source and destination exist afterward. mv moves — the source disappears from its old location and appears in the new one. This distinction matters when you need the original to stay.

definition.prompt: How do you rename a file using the command line?
options: Use the `rename` command
Use `mv` with the same directory but a different filename
Use `cp` and then delete the original
Use `ls --rename`
definition.explanation: There's no separate rename command in bash. Renaming is just moving a file to the same directory with a new name. `mv old.txt new.txt` renames old.txt to new.txt — same location, different name.

usage.prompt: You want to copy an entire project folder called `myapp/` into a backup called `myapp-backup/`. What flag does cp need?
options: -f (force)
-r (recursive)
-a (all)
-d (directory)
usage.explanation: cp -r copies directories recursively — it traverses all subdirectories and copies their contents too. Without -r, cp refuses to copy a directory and returns an error.

usage.prompt: You accidentally `mv` a file to the wrong location. How do you undo it?
options: Press Ctrl+Z
Run `mv --undo`
Run `mv` again with source and destination swapped
mv cannot be undone
usage.explanation: mv has no undo. But since mv is just a move, you can move it back by running mv again with the source and destination reversed. If you moved it to the wrong place, move it back to where it should be.

anatomy.prompt: In `cp -r src/ backup/`, what role does `src/` play?
options: The destination — where files will be copied to
A flag that tells cp to copy only source files
The source — the directory being copied
An optional modifier that enables fast copy mode
anatomy.explanation: Both cp and mv follow the pattern: `command [flags] SOURCE DESTINATION`. The first path argument is always the source (what to copy), and the second is the destination (where to put it). In `cp -r src/ backup/`, src/ is the source and backup/ is where the copy will go.

build.prompt: You want to make a backup of your .env file called .env.backup before editing it. Which command does this?
options: `mv .env .env.backup`
`cp .env .env.backup`
`cp -r .env .env.backup`
`cp .env.backup .env`
build.explanation: `cp .env .env.backup` copies .env to a new file called .env.backup. The original .env remains untouched. If you used mv, the original would be gone — then you'd have a backup but no working .env.

name: rm
what_it_is: `rm` deletes files permanently. There's no Trash, no undo — the file is gone immediately. `rm -r` removes a directory and everything inside it. `rm -f` skips any confirmation prompts, even on write-protected files. `rm -rf` combines both: no warnings, removes folders and all contents recursively. It's one of the most powerful and unforgiving commands in the shell.
analogy: Like throwing something directly into a furnace — not a trash can. There's no taking it back. The command prompt gives you zero warning. A misplaced `rm -rf` has deleted entire projects, production databases, and in famous cases, entire servers. The shell assumes you mean what you type.

examples.text: `rm file.txt` — deletes a single file. No output on success. No confirmation. No recovery. One command and it's gone permanently.

`rm -rf node_modules/` — removes the entire `node_modules` folder and all of its contents. This is actually one of the safest uses of `-rf` because `node_modules` is always regenerated by `npm install`. It's often the first step when you need to clean up a broken dependency install.

`rm -rf ./` — do not run this. This removes everything in the current directory and all subdirectories. There's no confirmation prompt. This is how projects get accidentally deleted. Always double-check the path before using -rf.

use_when: Cleaning up files you don't need, deleting old build artifacts, or removing node_modules to do a clean reinstall. Always verify the path before using -rf — especially if you used a variable to construct it.

definition.prompt: What makes `rm` different from deleting a file in a file manager?
options: rm is slower than clicking delete in a file manager
rm is permanent — there's no trash or undo; the file is gone immediately
rm requires sudo to delete any file
rm only works on text files
definition.explanation: When you delete via a file manager, the file usually goes to Trash — recoverable. rm bypasses that entirely. The file is immediately removed from the file system with no recovery option. This is why rm -rf is considered dangerous.

definition.prompt: What does `rm -rf` do?
options: Removes files but keeps directories intact
Removes files and directories recursively without asking for confirmation
Removes only read-only files
Renames files to a .deleted extension before removing
definition.explanation: -r makes rm recursive (handles directories and their contents), -f forces it without confirmation prompts. Combined, -rf removes anything you point it at — files, folders, everything inside them — silently and immediately.

usage.prompt: You want to do a clean reinstall of your npm packages. Which command safely removes node_modules?
options: `rm package.json`
`rm -rf node_modules/`
`rm node_modules`
`rm -r .`
usage.explanation: `rm -rf node_modules/` is one of the safest uses of -rf because node_modules can always be regenerated with npm install. Plain `rm node_modules` would fail because node_modules is a directory. `rm -r .` would delete your entire current directory — don't do that.

usage.prompt: Before running `rm -rf $path`, a developer double-checks the value of $path. Why?
options: rm only works with literal paths, not variables
If $path is empty or wrong, rm -rf could delete the wrong directory — potentially everything
Variables slow down rm's performance
rm -rf requires quotes around variables
usage.explanation: If $path is empty, `rm -rf $path` becomes `rm -rf` — which on some systems will delete the current directory. If it contains an unexpected path, you delete the wrong thing. Always verify variables before using them in destructive commands.

anatomy.prompt: In `rm -rf /tmp/build/`, what does the -r flag specifically enable?
options: Real-time deletion — shows progress
Remote deletion — works over SSH
Recursive deletion — removes directories and all their contents
Reverse deletion — removes files in reverse alphabetical order
anatomy.explanation: Without -r, rm can only delete individual files — it refuses to touch directories. -r enables recursive mode, which traverses into subdirectories and removes everything inside. It's required any time the target is a directory.

name: cat
what_it_is: `cat` prints the contents of a file directly in the terminal. The name stands for "concatenate" (you can string multiple files together), but in practice it's mostly used to quickly read a single file without opening an editor. Think of it as an instant preview — what's actually in this file, right now.
analogy: Like reading a book aloud — cat just speaks the file's contents directly into the terminal. You don't interact with it, you don't edit it, it just dumps the text so you can read or pipe it elsewhere.

examples.text: `cat package.json` — instantly shows the contents of your project's config file in the terminal. Useful when you want to quickly check a version number or confirm a script exists, without switching to your editor.
`cat .env` — shows all your environment variables at once. Good for debugging when a variable isn't being picked up — you can confirm it's in the file and spelled correctly.
`cat error.log | grep 'TypeError'` — print the log file and pipe it to grep to filter for only lines containing 'TypeError'. cat feeds the content; grep filters it.

example_command:
cat package.json
# Prints the full contents of package.json to the terminal
