definition.prompt: What are the two parts of every HTTP interaction?
options: Upload and download
Request and response
Headers and body
Client and database
definition.explanation: Every HTTP interaction is a pair: the client sends a request (with a method, URL, headers, and optional body), and the server sends back a response (with a status code, headers, and usually data).

name: HTTP Status Codes
what_it_is: Every HTTP response comes with a three-digit status code telling you whether it worked and why. The first digit gives the category: 2xx means success, 3xx means redirect, 4xx means the client made an error, 5xx means the server failed. You'll see these constantly in the browser Network tab, API responses, and error logs.
analogy: The expressions on a waiter's face when they bring back your order — a smile (200), 'we're out of that' (404), 'you didn't order anything' (400), or 'the kitchen is on fire' (500).

examples.text: `200 OK` — the request worked perfectly. `201 Created` — it worked and something new was created (common after a POST). `204 No Content` — it worked but there's nothing to return. All 2xx codes mean success.

`404 Not Found` — the server couldn't find what you asked for (wrong URL or missing resource). `401 Unauthorized` — you need to be logged in. `403 Forbidden` — you're logged in but not allowed. `400 Bad Request` — something was wrong with your request.

`500 Internal Server Error` — something went wrong on the server, not your fault. `502 Bad Gateway` — the server got a bad response from another server it depends on. These appear when a service is broken or overloaded.

use_when: Reading the Network tab in DevTools, handling API responses in code, or diagnosing why a request is failing.

definition.prompt: What category of HTTP status code indicates success?
options: 1xx
2xx
4xx
5xx
definition.explanation: 2xx status codes mean the request succeeded. 200 OK is the most common, but 201 (Created) and 204 (No Content) are also success responses with slightly different meanings.

definition.prompt: What does a 404 status code mean?
options: The server crashed
You need to log in
The requested resource was not found
The request was successful
definition.explanation: 404 Not Found means the server understood your request but couldn't find the resource you asked for — usually a wrong URL path or a resource that doesn't exist.

definition.prompt: If an API returns 401, what does that typically mean?
options: The server is down
You are not authenticated — you need to log in or provide an API key
The resource was not found
Your request body was malformed
definition.explanation: 401 Unauthorized means authentication is required but wasn't provided or was invalid. The server knows who you need to be, but you haven't proved it yet.

name: DNS
what_it_is: DNS (Domain Name System) converts a domain name like `github.com` into the IP address computers actually use to connect. Your computer communicates using IP addresses (like `140.82.113.3`), but humans use domain names. DNS is the phonebook that translates between them. Every time you visit a website, a DNS lookup happens in milliseconds before your browser sends a single byte of data.
analogy: A phonebook — you look up a name (github.com) and get back the number (IP address) your computer needs to actually make the call.

examples.text: When you visit `github.com`, your browser first asks a DNS resolver "what IP address is github.com?" The resolver returns something like `140.82.113.3`. Your browser then connects to that IP. You never see this — it's automatic and takes milliseconds.

When you buy a domain and point it at a hosting provider, you're setting DNS records — telling the world which IP address requests for your domain should go to. Changes to DNS records can take hours to propagate because they're cached.
`nslookup github.com` in the terminal performs a DNS lookup and shows the resolved IP address. Useful for diagnosing whether a domain is resolving correctly or pointing to the expected server.

use_when: When setting up a custom domain on a hosting provider, debugging why a site isn't loading, or understanding why DNS changes take time to take effect.

definition.prompt: What does DNS do?
options: Encrypts web traffic
Converts domain names to IP addresses
Stores your website's files
Routes packets across the internet
definition.explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names like `github.com` into the IP addresses computers use to actually connect. Without DNS, you'd have to memorize IP addresses for every site.

definition.prompt: Why do DNS record changes sometimes take hours to take effect?
options: Servers need to restart to read new records
DNS records are cached by resolvers and take time to expire
GitHub has to manually approve changes
DNS is only updated once per day
definition.explanation: DNS responses are cached by resolvers (and your OS) with a TTL (time to live). Until that cache expires, old records are returned. This is why new DNS records can take hours to propagate globally.

definition.prompt: What terminal command performs a DNS lookup for a domain?
options: dns-lookup
ping --resolve
nslookup
curl --dns
definition.explanation: `nslookup` queries DNS and shows the resolved IP address for a domain. `dig` is a more detailed alternative. Both are standard tools for diagnosing DNS issues.

name: Client & Server
what_it_is: The client is whatever is making the request — usually a browser, but also a mobile app, a terminal command, or your own code. The server is what receives the request and sends back a response — a computer running software built to handle those requests. The entire web runs on millions of these client/server exchanges happening simultaneously, and understanding which side you're on matters when you're writing or debugging code.
analogy: A client is anyone placing an order; a server is the kitchen filling it. Your app can be both — a server to users, a client to other APIs.

examples.text: When you use a weather app, your phone is the client — it sends a request to a weather API server asking for today's forecast. The server looks it up and sends back the data. Your phone displays it. Classic client/server.

"Client-side" code runs in the browser on the user's machine — HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. "Server-side" code runs on the server — Node.js, Python, etc. Client-side code is visible to users; server-side code is not.

When your Node.js app makes a `fetch` call to an external API, your Node.js app is the client — even though it's running on a server. "Client" just means whoever is initiating the request, regardless of where it's running.

use_when: Understanding where code runs, who is responsible when a request fails, and why secrets should never be in client-side code.

definition.prompt: In a web context, what is a 'client'?
options: A paying customer of a software company
Whatever is sending the request — browser, app, or code
The database that stores user data
The server's operating system
definition.explanation: A client is anything that initiates a request — a browser loading a page, a mobile app fetching data, or backend code calling an external API. The client starts the conversation.

definition.prompt: Where does 'client-side' code run?
options: On the server in a data center
In the browser on the user's machine
In a Docker container
In a database
definition.explanation: Client-side code runs in the browser on the user's device. This includes HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Because it runs on the user's machine, users can inspect it — which is why secrets don't belong there.

definition.prompt: Can a server also act as a client?
options: No — servers only receive requests
Yes — when it makes requests to other APIs or services
Only if it's running Node.js
Only for internal requests
definition.explanation: A server is just software that handles requests. When that same server calls an external API or database, it's acting as a client. The terms describe the role in a specific request, not the machine.

name: The DOM
what_it_is: The DOM (Document Object Model) is how the browser represents an HTML page in memory — a live tree of objects, one per element. When JavaScript changes the page (adds a button, updates text, hides a div), it's modifying the DOM. The DOM is not the HTML file — it's what the browser built from reading that file, and it can change dynamically after the page loads.
analogy: The HTML file is the blueprint; the DOM is the actual building. JavaScript can renovate the building while it's occupied — without touching the blueprint.

examples.text: `document.getElementById('title').textContent = 'Hello'` changes the DOM — it finds the element with id `title` in the live tree and updates its text. The HTML file on disk is unchanged; only the live page has changed.

In DevTools → Elements tab, you're looking at the DOM — not the original HTML source. If JavaScript added or removed elements since the page loaded, the DOM will look different from the HTML file you wrote.

React and Vue don't directly touch the DOM for every update. They maintain a virtual DOM (a copy in JS memory) and only update the real DOM when something actually changed — which is faster than rewriting the whole page.

use_when: When writing JavaScript that interacts with the page, debugging why an element isn't showing up, or understanding how React's rendering model works.

definition.prompt: What is the DOM?
options: The HTML file as it exists on disk
The browser's live in-memory representation of the current page
A JavaScript library for building UIs
A type of database
definition.explanation: The DOM (Document Object Model) is the browser's live, in-memory tree of objects representing the current page. JavaScript manipulates the DOM to change what the user sees — not the original HTML file.

definition.prompt: If JavaScript adds a new button to the page, where does that button exist?
options: In the original HTML file
Only in the DOM — the browser's in-memory representation
In both the HTML file and the DOM
In local storage
definition.explanation: JavaScript changes the DOM — the live in-memory tree. The original HTML file on the server is untouched. If you reload the page, the HTML is re-parsed and the DOM is rebuilt without the JavaScript-added button.

definition.prompt: In DevTools Elements tab, are you looking at the original HTML or the current DOM?
options: The original HTML file from the server
The current DOM — which may differ from the original HTML if JavaScript has run
A cached version from the last reload
The minified production HTML
definition.explanation: DevTools Elements shows the live DOM — the current state of the page, including any changes JavaScript has made since the page loaded. It's often different from the original HTML source.

name: Browser DevTools
what_it_is: DevTools is a set of debugging and inspection tools built into every browser — open it with F12 or right-click → Inspect. It lets you view and edit HTML/CSS live, run JavaScript in the console, watch every network request in real time, see errors, and inspect storage. It's the most important tool for web development that isn't a code editor, and it's free in every browser.
analogy: An X-ray and control panel for any web page — you can see inside it, change things live, and watch everything happening in real time.

examples.text: In the Elements tab, click any element on the page to see its HTML and CSS. You can edit styles live and see the changes instantly — great for experimenting with CSS without touching your actual code files.

In the Network tab, every request the page makes appears in real time — API calls, images, scripts — with the URL, status code, and full response. If an API call is failing or returning wrong data, this is where you find it.

The Console tab shows errors and `console.log()` output from your JavaScript. If something's broken on a page, open DevTools and check the console first — there's almost always an error message pointing directly at the problem.

use_when: Debugging layout issues (Elements), inspecting API calls (Network), or tracing JavaScript errors (Console) — open it constantly while building for the web.

definition.prompt: How do you open browser DevTools?
options: By installing a browser extension
By pressing F12 or right-clicking and choosing Inspect
By typing 'devtools' in the address bar
Only available in developer builds of browsers
definition.explanation: DevTools is built into every modern browser. F12 opens it instantly, or you can right-click any element and choose Inspect to open it focused on that element.

definition.prompt: Which DevTools tab shows all network requests a page makes, including API calls?
options: Elements
Console
Network
Sources
definition.explanation: The Network tab logs every HTTP request the page makes — page loads, API calls, images, fonts — with the URL, method, status code, and full response body.

definition.prompt: Where in DevTools would you look to see JavaScript errors and console.log() output?
options: Network tab
Elements tab
Console tab
Storage tab
definition.explanation: The Console tab displays JavaScript runtime errors, warnings, and any output from `console.log()`. It's usually the first place to check when debugging unexpected behavior.

name: Local Storage & Cookies
what_it_is: Browsers give websites small amounts of storage on your device to remember things between visits. Cookies are small key-value pairs sent automatically with every request to the server — used for sessions, logins, and tracking. Local Storage is a larger key-value store that lives in the browser only and is never automatically sent to the server — used for saving preferences, cached data, or app state between sessions.
analogy: Cookies are a name badge you wear to every meeting so the host knows who you are. Local Storage is a notebook in your pocket you write in yourself — the host never sees it.

examples.text: When you log into a site and it remembers you next time, it stored a session cookie. Every request you send to that site includes that cookie automatically — the server reads it to confirm who you are without asking you to log in again.
`localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark')` stores a value. When the page loads later, `localStorage.getItem('theme')` reads it back. The preference survives closing the tab — no server involved.
In DevTools → Application tab, you can inspect all cookies and Local Storage for the current site. Clearing them is how you manually log out or reset an app's saved state when debugging.

use_when: Saving user preferences client-side, debugging login issues, or understanding why a site "remembers" you between visits.

definition.prompt: What is the key difference between cookies and Local Storage?
options: Cookies hold more data than Local Storage
Cookies are sent to the server with every request; Local Storage stays in the browser only
Local Storage expires automatically; cookies don't
Cookies require HTTPS; Local Storage doesn't
definition.explanation: Cookies are automatically included in every HTTP request to the matching domain — the server sees them. Local Storage is browser-only; nothing sends it to the server automatically. This makes them suited for different purposes.

definition.prompt: What is a session cookie typically used for?
options: Storing large amounts of user data
Keeping you logged in by sending your session ID with each request
Caching images for faster load times
Storing your local timezone
definition.explanation: After login, the server creates a session and gives you a session cookie containing the session ID. Your browser sends that cookie with every subsequent request, so the server knows you're authenticated.

definition.prompt: Where in DevTools can you view a site's cookies and Local Storage?
options: Network tab
Console tab
Application tab
Elements tab
definition.explanation: DevTools → Application tab shows all storage for the current site: cookies, Local Storage, Session Storage, IndexedDB, and more. You can view, edit, and delete values directly from there.

name: HTML Structure
what_it_is:
HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the language that defines what's on a web page — the content and its structure. Everything is built from elements: tags like `

`, `

`, `

`, `
analogy: HTML is the skeleton — it defines what body parts exist and how they connect. CSS is the skin and clothes; JavaScript is the muscles.

examples.text:
`

Hello

` tells the browser "this is a top-level heading with the text Hello." The `

` tag signals importance and structure — browsers style it large by default, but that's CSS doing the visual work.

HTML elements nest inside each other to create hierarchy. A `<nav>` contains `<a>` links; a `<ul>` contains `<li>` items; a `<form>` contains `<input>` and `<button>` elements. This nesting is the structure the DOM tree represents.

Semantic HTML means using the right tag for the job — `<article>` instead of just `<div>`, `<button>` instead of a styled `<span>`. Screen readers and search engines use element types to understand page structure, not just its appearance.

use_when: Building any web page — HTML is the foundation everything else is layered on top of.

definition.prompt: What does HTML define in a web page?
options: How the page looks — colors, fonts, and layout
The content and structure — what elements exist and how they relate
How the page behaves when a user clicks something
The server-side logic for handling data
definition.explanation: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) defines the content and structure of a page — what's there and what type each thing is. CSS handles appearance; JavaScript handles behavior.

definition.prompt: What does the `<h1>` tag tell the browser?
options: Make this text very large
This is the most important heading on the page
Center this text
This text should be bold
definition.explanation: `<h1>` signals that this is the top-level heading — a structural role, not a visual instruction. Browsers apply default styling to headings, but the semantic meaning is 'this is the main heading.'

definition.prompt: What is 'semantic HTML'?
options: HTML written without any CSS
Using the right tag for its purpose — nav, article, button — rather than generic divs
HTML that loads faster
HTML optimized for mobile devices
definition.explanation: Semantic HTML uses meaningful tags that describe content purpose — `<nav>`, `<article>`, `<footer>`, `<button>`. This helps screen readers, search engines, and other developers understand the page structure.

name: CSS
what_it_is: CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is what makes a web page look like something. You write rules that target HTML elements and describe how they should appear — color, size, spacing, layout, animation. Without CSS, every web page would be unstyled black text on a white background. CSS also makes pages responsive, adapting how content looks across different screen sizes.
analogy: If HTML is the skeleton, CSS is the skin, clothing, and makeup — it controls every visual aspect of how the structure looks.

examples.text:
`h1 { color: #e06c75; font-size: 32px; }` targets every `

` on the page and makes it red and large. The HTML still just says `

Hello

` — CSS is a completely separate layer that handles all the visuals.

Media queries let you write different CSS for different screen sizes: `@media (max-width: 768px) { ... }` applies those styles only on screens narrower than 768px. This is how responsive design works — one page, different layouts.
CSS classes let you define reusable styles. `.btn { padding: 12px 24px; background: #61afef; }` can be applied to any HTML element with `class="btn"` — one definition, used everywhere.

use_when: Any time you want to control the visual appearance of a web page — layout, color, typography, spacing, or responsiveness.

definition.prompt: What is CSS responsible for in a web page?
options: Defining what content exists on the page
Controlling the visual appearance — layout, color, spacing, typography
Handling user interactions and logic
Fetching data from a server
definition.explanation: CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) handles everything visual — how HTML elements look, how they're positioned, how they respond to different screen sizes. HTML defines structure; CSS styles it.

definition.prompt: What does `h1 { color: red; }` do?
options: Creates an h1 element with red text
Makes all h1 elements on the page have red text
Changes the HTML file to add a color attribute
Only affects the first h1 on the page
definition.explanation: A CSS rule like `h1 { color: red; }` applies to every `<h1>` element on the page — it targets all of them unless a more specific rule overrides it for a particular one.

definition.prompt: What CSS feature lets you apply different styles on mobile vs desktop screens?
options: CSS variables
Media queries
Flexbox
CSS Grid
definition.explanation: Media queries (`@media`) let you write CSS that only applies when the screen meets certain conditions — like a maximum width. This is the foundation of responsive design.

name: The Box Model
what_it_is: Every HTML element is a rectangle — even text. The box model describes the four layers that make up that rectangle: content (the text or image itself), padding (space between the content and the border), border (the outline), and margin (space outside the border that pushes other elements away). Understanding the box model is what makes CSS layout feel predictable instead of guesswork.
analogy: A picture frame — the photo is the content, the mat is padding, the frame is the border, and the wall space around it is the margin.

examples.text: A button that looks too cramped: add `padding: 12px 24px` to add breathing room between the text and the edge. Add `margin: 16px` to push other elements away from the outside of the button. Two different fixes for two different spacing problems.

`box-sizing: border-box` makes width work intuitively — the width you set includes padding and border. Without it, padding is added on top of the width, making elements wider than you intended. Most modern projects set this globally.

In DevTools Elements panel, hover over an element and a colored box model diagram appears — showing the exact pixel values of content, padding, border, and margin. Invaluable for debugging why an element is the wrong size.

use_when: Whenever CSS layout doesn't look right — spacing too tight, elements the wrong size, or things not aligning — the box model is usually involved.

definition.prompt: What are the four layers of the CSS box model, from innermost to outermost?
options: Content, border, padding, margin
Content, padding, border, margin
Margin, border, padding, content
Text, spacing, outline, gap
definition.explanation: From inside out: content (the actual text/image), padding (inner spacing), border (the outline), margin (outer spacing). Each layer can be set independently.

definition.prompt: You want space between a button's text and its border. Which property do you change?
options: margin
padding
border
gap
definition.explanation: Padding is the space inside the border — between the content and the edge. To add breathing room inside a button (between the label and the edge), you increase padding.

definition.prompt: What does `box-sizing: border-box` change?
options: Makes the border invisible
Includes padding and border inside the set width, instead of adding to it
Removes the margin from the box model
Makes the element's size fluid
definition.explanation: By default (`content-box`), `width` only sets the content area — padding and border are added on top. `border-box` makes `width` include padding and border, so the element is exactly as wide as you set.

name: CSS Specificity & the Cascade
what_it_is: When two CSS rules target the same element and set conflicting styles, the browser uses specificity to decide which one wins. More specific selectors beat less specific ones — an ID (`#header`) beats a class (`.header`) which beats a tag (`h1`). The "cascade" is the set of rules for resolving these conflicts. This is why adding a style sometimes seems to have no effect — another rule with higher specificity is overriding it.
analogy: A chain of command — a direct order from a general (ID) overrides an order from a captain (class), which overrides a standing rule (tag).

examples.text: You write `h1 { color: blue; }` but the heading is still red. Look for a more specific rule targeting the same element — probably `.page-header h1 { color: red; }`. The class-qualified selector wins because it's more specific than just `h1`.
Inline styles (`style="color: red"` on the element) beat any external CSS rule — they have the highest specificity short of `!important`. Both are hard to maintain; use them only when necessary.

Specificity is scored: inline styles = 1000, IDs = 100, classes/attributes = 10, tags = 1. `#nav .link a` scores 111. A rule with a higher score always wins, regardless of which comes first in the file.

use_when: When a CSS rule you wrote isn't taking effect — specificity is almost always the reason.

definition.prompt: What is CSS specificity?
options: How fast a CSS rule loads
The rule that determines which CSS style wins when two rules conflict
How many elements a CSS rule targets
The order CSS files are loaded in
definition.explanation: Specificity is the scoring system CSS uses to resolve conflicts between rules targeting the same element. More specific selectors override less specific ones — regardless of the order they appear in the file.

definition.prompt: Which selector has the highest specificity?
options: A tag selector like `h1`
A class selector like `.header`
An ID selector like `#header`
A descendant selector like `div p`
definition.explanation: ID selectors have a specificity score of 100 — far higher than class selectors (10) or tag selectors (1). An ID rule will always beat a class or tag rule targeting the same property.

definition.prompt: Your CSS rule isn't applying even though it looks correct. What's the most likely cause?
options: The browser doesn't support that CSS property
A more specific rule elsewhere is overriding it
CSS rules only apply after a page reload
The property name has a typo
definition.explanation: When a style doesn't apply as expected, a more specific rule targeting the same element is almost always overriding it. Use DevTools Elements tab to see all competing rules and which one is winning.

name: Flexbox
what_it_is: Flexbox is a CSS layout system for arranging items in a row or column — controlling how they're spaced, aligned, and sized relative to each other. You add `display: flex` to a container and its direct children become flex items you can control with a handful of properties. Before Flexbox, centering things in CSS was notoriously painful. Now it's two lines.
analogy: A smart shelf system — you set the rules (direction, spacing, alignment) and it arranges everything inside automatically, adjusting as items are added or removed.

examples.text: `display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center;` — three properties that put items in a row, spread them evenly across the width, and center them vertically. This combination appears in almost every navigation bar ever built.

`flex: 1` on a child element means "take up as much remaining space as possible." Two siblings both with `flex: 1` split the space equally. One with `flex: 2` gets twice as much as one with `flex: 1`.

Flexbox is one-dimensional — it handles a row or a column, not both at once. For two-dimensional layout (rows AND columns), use CSS Grid. In practice most UIs use both: Grid for the overall page layout, Flexbox for components.

use_when: Aligning items in a row or column — navbars, button groups, card layouts, centering content — Flexbox handles most single-axis layout problems cleanly.

definition.prompt: What does adding `display: flex` to a container do?
options: Makes the container take up the full screen width
Enables Flexbox layout — making direct children into flex items you can arrange
Hides the container until it has content
Makes child elements all the same size
definition.explanation: `display: flex` activates Flexbox on the container. Its direct children become flex items that respond to properties like `justify-content`, `align-items`, and `flex`.

definition.prompt: What does `justify-content: space-between` do in a flex container?
options: Adds equal space inside each flex item
Centers all flex items in the middle
Distributes flex items with space between them, first and last at the edges
Makes all flex items the same width
definition.explanation: `space-between` places the first item at the start edge, the last at the end edge, and distributes remaining space evenly between the items in the middle — a classic navigation bar pattern.

definition.prompt: Flexbox is best described as a layout system for which dimension?
options: Two dimensions — rows and columns simultaneously
One dimension — either a row or a column
Grid-based layouts with fixed columns
Full-page layouts with header and sidebar
definition.explanation: Flexbox is one-dimensional — it lays things out in either a row (`flex-direction: row`) or a column (`flex-direction: column`). For two-dimensional layouts, CSS Grid is the right tool.

name: Variables & Data Types
what_it_is: A variable is a named container that holds a value — you give it a name, assign something to it, and refer to it by that name later. Every piece of data your code works with lives in a variable. Data types describe what kind of value it is: text (string), a number, true/false (boolean), or the absence of a value (null/undefined). In JavaScript, `let` and `const` are the modern ways to declare variables.
analogy: A labeled box — you write a name on the outside, put something inside, and refer to it by the label whenever you need it.

examples.text: `const name = 'Alex'` — a string variable. `let age = 28` — a number variable. `const isLoggedIn = true` — a boolean. Use `const` when the value won't be reassigned; `let` when it will.
`null` means "intentionally empty" — you assigned it nothing on purpose. `undefined` means the variable was declared but never assigned a value. Both mean "no value," but for different reasons.

JavaScript is loosely typed — you don't declare the type, it's inferred. `typeof 'hello'` returns `'string'`; `typeof 42` returns `'number'`. The type can change if you reassign a `let` variable with a different kind of value.

use_when: Always — variables are the basic unit of storage in any program. When AI-generated code breaks, variable names and types are often the first thing to check.

definition.prompt: What is a variable in programming?
options: A type of loop
A named container that stores a value
A file that holds configuration
A function that returns a value
definition.explanation: A variable is a named storage location. You give it a name, assign it a value, and use that name to access or change the value later in your program.

definition.prompt: When should you use `const` instead of `let`?
options: When the value is a string
When the value won't be reassigned after it's set
When the variable is inside a function
When the value is a number
definition.explanation: `const` means the binding is constant — you can't reassign it. Use it by default and only reach for `let` when you know the value will change (like a counter or a flag you'll toggle).

definition.prompt: What is the difference between `null` and `undefined`?
options: They are identical — both mean no value
null means intentionally empty; undefined means a variable was declared but never assigned
undefined means the variable doesn't exist; null means it does
null is for numbers; undefined is for strings
definition.explanation: `null` is an explicit assignment — the developer chose to set the value to 'nothing.' `undefined` happens automatically when a variable is declared but no value is given to it.

name: Functions
what_it_is: A function is a named, reusable block of code that does a specific job. You define it once, then call it by name whenever you need it. Functions can accept inputs (called parameters) and give back an output (called a return value). They're how you avoid repeating yourself — instead of writing the same logic five times, you write it once and call the function five times.
analogy: A recipe — you write down the steps once, give it a name, and follow it whenever you need it. The ingredients are the parameters; the finished dish is the return value.

examples.text: `function greet(name) { return 'Hello, ' + name; }` — defines a function called `greet` that takes one parameter and returns a string. `greet('Alex')` calls it and gets back `'Hello, Alex'`.
Arrow functions are a shorthand: `const double = (n) => n * 2`. Same idea — takes a number, returns the doubled value. Arrow functions are common in modern JavaScript and in code AI tools generate.
Functions without a `return` statement return `undefined`. A common bug: you write a function to calculate something but forget `return`, then wonder why the result is always `undefined`.

use_when: Any time you have a block of logic you'll use more than once, or any time you want to give a piece of logic a meaningful name.

definition.prompt: What is a function in programming?
options: A type of variable
A named, reusable block of code that performs a specific task
A loop that runs a set number of times
A file that exports values
definition.explanation: A function is a named block of code you can call by name. It takes optional inputs (parameters), does its work, and optionally returns a value. The key benefit is reusability — define once, use everywhere.

definition.prompt: What are the inputs you pass into a function called?
options: Variables
Returns
Parameters (or arguments)
Outputs
definition.explanation: The values you define in the function signature are called parameters. The values you actually pass when calling the function are called arguments. Most people use the terms interchangeably.

definition.prompt: What does a function return if it has no `return` statement?
options: null
undefined
An empty string
definition.explanation: In JavaScript, a function with no `return` statement (or just `return;`) returns `undefined`. This is a common source of bugs when you forget to return a calculated value.

name: Scope
what_it_is: Scope determines where a variable is accessible. A variable declared inside a function only exists inside that function — code outside can't see it. A variable at the top level of a file is accessible everywhere in that file. This is called local vs global scope. Scope is why you sometimes get a "not defined" error even though you can see the variable name right there — it exists, just not where you're trying to use it.
analogy: What's said in the kitchen stays in the kitchen — a variable declared inside a function can't be heard by code outside that function.

examples.text: A variable declared inside a function with `let` or `const` is local — it only exists while the function runs and disappears after. Code outside the function can't access it, even by name.

`let` and `const` are block-scoped — they're only accessible inside the `{}` block where they're declared. `var` is function-scoped (older behavior) and ignores block boundaries, which is why modern code uses `let`/`const` instead.

Closures are when a function "remembers" variables from its outer scope even after that outer function has finished running. This is a common pattern in event handlers and callbacks — the inner function keeps access to variables from where it was created.

use_when: When you get a 'not defined' error on a variable you can see in the code, or when a variable change in one place isn't affecting another place you expected it to.

definition.prompt: What does 'scope' determine in programming?
options: How fast a variable is accessed
Where in the code a variable is accessible
What data type a variable holds
How many times a function can be called
definition.explanation: Scope defines the region of code where a variable exists and can be referenced. Variables declared inside a function are local to it; variables at the top level are accessible more broadly.

definition.prompt: Why might you get a 'ReferenceError: x is not defined' even though you can see `x` in the code?
options: The variable name is too short
x is declared in a different scope than where you're trying to use it
x is the wrong data type
JavaScript is case sensitive
definition.explanation: If `x` is declared inside a function or block and you try to access it outside, JavaScript can't find it — even though it exists in the source code. It exists, just not in the current scope.

definition.prompt: What is the key difference between `let`/`const` and `var` regarding scope?
options: var is faster than let/const
let/const are block-scoped; var is function-scoped and leaks out of blocks
var can hold more data than let
let/const only work inside functions
definition.explanation: `let` and `const` respect block boundaries (`{}`). `var` only respects function boundaries — it leaks out of `if` blocks, loops, and similar, causing unexpected access. This is why `var` is avoided in modern code.

name: Arrays
what_it_is: An array is an ordered list of values. Items are stored in sequence and accessed by their position (called an index), starting at 0. Arrays can hold anything — strings, numbers, objects, even other arrays. They come with built-in methods for adding, removing, searching, and transforming the items inside. Most data you work with in real apps — lists of users, search results, menu items — arrives and lives as an array.
analogy: A numbered list — each item has a position starting at 0, and you can add, remove, or look up any item by its position number.

examples.text: `const fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'mango']` — an array of three strings. `fruits[0]` gives `'apple'` (index 0), `fruits.length` gives `3`. Arrays are zero-indexed — the first item is always at position 0.
`fruits.push('grape')` adds to the end. `fruits.pop()` removes from the end. `fruits.includes('banana')` returns `true` or `false`. These are the most-used array methods.

`fruits.map(f => f.toUpperCase())` returns a new array with every item uppercased. `fruits.filter(f => f.length > 5)` returns only items with more than 5 characters. `map` and `filter` are the workhorses of data transformation in JS.

use_when: Storing and working with any ordered collection of items — API responses, form options, search results, lists of anything.

definition.prompt: What index does the first item in an array have?
options: It depends on the array
definition.explanation: Arrays are zero-indexed — the first item is at index 0, the second at 1, and so on. `array[0]` always gives you the first element.

definition.prompt: What does `array.push(value)` do?
options: Removes the last item
Adds a value to the beginning
Adds a value to the end
Returns the array length
definition.explanation: `push()` adds one or more values to the end of an array and returns the new length. It modifies the original array in place.

definition.prompt: What does `array.map(fn)` return?
options: The original array, modified in place
A new array with each item transformed by the function
The first item matching the function
The count of items matching the function
definition.explanation: `map()` creates a new array by running every item through a function and collecting the results. The original array is untouched. It's the standard way to transform a list of data.

name: Objects
what_it_is: An object is a collection of key-value pairs — named properties that each hold a value. Instead of accessing data by position (like an array), you access it by name. Objects are everywhere in JavaScript: JSON responses from APIs are objects, React props are objects, the browser's `window` is an object. Anything with named properties is an object.
analogy: A form filled out — each field has a label (key) and what you wrote in it (value). You look up "email" to get the email, "name" to get the name.

examples.text: `const user = { name: 'Alex', age: 28, isAdmin: false }` — an object with three properties. `user.name` gives `'Alex'`. `user['age']` also gives `28`. Both dot notation and bracket notation work.

You can add new properties any time: `user.email = 'alex@example.com'`. And read nested objects: `user.address.city` accesses the `city` property inside a nested `address` object — but only if `address` exists first or you'll get an error.

API responses are almost always objects or arrays of objects. `response.data.users[0].name` is a typical real-world access pattern — drilling into a nested structure to get to the value you actually need.

use_when: Representing structured data — a user, a product, a config — where each piece of data has a name. Also for working with JSON from any API.

definition.prompt: What is a JavaScript object?
options: An ordered list of values accessed by index
A collection of named key-value pairs
A function that returns a value
A type of loop
definition.explanation: An object is a collection of key-value pairs. Each property has a name (key) and a value. You access values by their name rather than by position.

definition.prompt: Given `const car = { make: 'Toyota', year: 2022 }`, how do you access the make?
options: car[0]
car.first
car.make or car['make']
car->make
definition.explanation: Object properties are accessed by name using dot notation (`car.make`) or bracket notation (`car['make']`). Both return `'Toyota'`. Bracket notation is needed when the property name is in a variable or contains special characters.

definition.prompt: What happens if you try to access a property that doesn't exist on an object?
options: An error is thrown
You get null
You get undefined
You get an empty string
definition.explanation: Accessing a non-existent property returns `undefined` — not an error. This can cause subtle bugs where you expect a value but silently get `undefined` and only notice later when something that depends on it fails.

name: Reading Error Messages
what_it_is: An error message tells you exactly what went wrong, what type of error it is, and — in most cases — exactly which file and line number caused it. Most beginners read the first line and give up. The real information is in the stack trace: the chain of function calls that led to the crash. Learning to read errors quickly is one of the highest-leverage skills in development.
analogy: A crash report with a map of how you got there — the error type is what happened, the stack trace is the turn-by-turn directions of every function call that led to the crash.

examples.text: An error message has three key parts: the error type (`TypeError`), the message (`Cannot read properties of undefined`), and the stack trace (the list of file + line numbers). Start at the top of the stack trace — that's where the crash actually happened.

When you see `at UserCard (UserCard.jsx:24)` in a stack trace, it means the crash occurred on line 24 of UserCard.jsx inside the UserCard function. Click the file:line reference in your browser's console and it jumps straight there.

Copy the full error message (type + message, not the stack trace) and paste it into an AI tool or search engine. Include the library or framework name for better results. The exact error text is almost always googleable.

use_when: Every time something breaks. Don't guess — read the error first. The message and line number almost always point directly at the problem.

definition.prompt: What is a stack trace?
options: A list of all variables in your program
The chain of function calls that led to the error
A log of every line your code has run
A list of all imported modules
definition.explanation: A stack trace shows the sequence of function calls that were active when the error occurred — from the outermost call down to the line that actually threw. It's a map of how execution got to the crash point.

definition.prompt: Where in the stack trace is the actual line that caused the crash?
options: At the bottom
At the top
In the middle
Stack traces don't show line numbers
definition.explanation: Stack traces are listed most-recent-first. The top entry is where the crash happened. Lower entries are the callers that led there — useful context but not the source of the problem.

definition.prompt: What's the most effective first step when your code throws an error?
options: Restart the dev server
Add more console.log statements
Read the error type and message, then check the file and line number it points to
Search for the function name on Stack Overflow
definition.explanation: Read the error first. The type tells you the category of problem; the message tells you the specifics; the file and line number tell you exactly where to look. Guessing or restarting before reading is wasted time.

name: Common Error Types
what_it_is: JavaScript has several named error types, and the name is the first clue about what went wrong. `SyntaxError` means the code can't even be parsed — a missing bracket or typo. `ReferenceError` means you used a variable name that doesn't exist in scope. `TypeError` means you tried to do something with a value that doesn't support it — like calling `.map()` on something that isn't an array. Recognizing these patterns saves you from having to read the full message every time.
analogy: Medical diagnosis categories — 'broken bone' narrows down the problem before you even look at the X-ray. Knowing the error type tells you roughly what category of mistake you made.

examples.text: `SyntaxError: Unexpected token ')'` — a typo or missing character in the code itself. The file won't run at all. Check the line number and look for a missing comma, bracket, or quote nearby.

`TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'name')` — you tried to access `.name` on something that was `undefined`. Usually means an API response hasn't loaded yet, or a variable wasn't set before you tried to use it.
`ReferenceError: user is not defined` — you used `user` but it doesn't exist in this scope. Either it was never declared, it's declared in a different scope, or there's a typo in the variable name.

use_when: As the first line of diagnosis when an error occurs — the error type immediately narrows down the category of problem.

definition.prompt: What kind of mistake causes a SyntaxError?
options: Using a variable from the wrong scope
The code can't be parsed — missing bracket, typo, or invalid syntax
Calling a method on the wrong data type
An API returning unexpected data
definition.explanation: A SyntaxError means JavaScript couldn't even parse (read) the code. The file won't run at all. Usually a missing bracket, comma, or quote. Check the line number and the surrounding characters.

definition.prompt: What does `TypeError: x.map is not a function` usually mean?
options: The function `map` doesn't exist in JavaScript
`x` is not an array — you're calling .map() on something that doesn't have that method
The map function has a bug
You need to import the map function first
definition.explanation: `.map()` is an Array method. If `x` is `undefined`, `null`, a string, or an object, it won't have `.map`. This error usually means the data you expected to be an array is actually something else.

definition.prompt: You get `ReferenceError: myData is not defined`. What's the most likely cause?
options: myData is the wrong data type
myData was declared in a scope this code can't access, or it was never declared
myData has a null value
JavaScript doesn't support that variable name
definition.explanation: ReferenceError means JavaScript can't find a variable with that name in the current scope. Either it was declared somewhere inaccessible (wrong scope), it was never declared, or there's a typo.

name: Try/Catch
what_it_is: Try/catch lets you handle errors gracefully instead of letting them crash your program. Code in the `try` block runs normally. If an error occurs, execution jumps to the `catch` block, which receives the error and can handle it — log it, show a message, retry, or fall back to a default. Without try/catch, one unexpected error crashes the whole operation.
analogy: A safety net under a tightrope — the performer tries the walk normally, but if they fall, the net catches them instead of letting them hit the ground.

examples.text: Wrapping an API call in try/catch means if the request fails, your app shows an error message instead of crashing. The `catch(err)` block receives the error object — `err.message` tells you what went wrong.

`finally` runs after try/catch regardless of whether an error occurred — useful for cleanup like hiding a loading spinner. It runs whether the try succeeded, the catch ran, or even if there was a `return` inside try.
For async/await code, you use `try/catch` directly around the `await`. For promise chains, `.catch()` serves the same purpose. Both catch rejected promises — use whichever fits the code style.

use_when: Around any code that might fail — API calls, JSON parsing, file reads, third-party libraries — anywhere an unexpected error would break the user experience.

definition.prompt: What is the purpose of try/catch?
options: To run code faster by skipping validation
To handle errors gracefully instead of letting them crash the program
To declare variables that might not be set
To retry failed operations automatically
definition.explanation: Try/catch lets you anticipate that something might go wrong and handle it. The try block attempts the operation; if it throws, execution jumps to catch where you can respond — log, recover, or show a user-friendly message.

definition.prompt: What happens if an error occurs inside a try block?
options: The program crashes immediately
The error is silently ignored
Execution jumps to the catch block with the error object
The try block retries automatically
definition.explanation: When an error is thrown inside try, JavaScript immediately stops executing the try block and jumps to catch, passing the error object. Code after the throw point in try is skipped.

definition.prompt: What does the `finally` block do?
options: Only runs if no error occurred
Only runs if an error occurred
Runs after try/catch regardless of whether an error occurred
Retries the try block on failure
definition.explanation: `finally` always runs — after the try block if it succeeded, or after the catch block if it failed. It's for cleanup that needs to happen either way, like hiding a loading state.

name: Synchronous vs Asynchronous
what_it_is: Synchronous code runs line by line — each line finishes before the next starts. Asynchronous code starts a task, moves on, and comes back when the task finishes. JavaScript is single-threaded (it can only do one thing at a time), so async is how it handles slow work — like waiting for an API response — without freezing the entire browser tab while it waits.
analogy: Synchronous is a single cashier who fully processes each customer before taking the next. Asynchronous is placing an order and getting a buzzer — you step aside while the kitchen works, and the cashier takes the next order.

examples.text: Fetching data from an API takes time — could be 50ms or 3 seconds. If JavaScript waited synchronously, the page would freeze completely during that time. Async lets JavaScript start the fetch, render the rest of the page, and update when the data arrives.

The classic symptom of async confusion: you fetch data, immediately `console.log` it on the next line, and get `undefined`. The fetch hadn't finished yet. The data arrives later — you need to wait for it properly with `await` or `.then()`.

JavaScript's event loop is what enables async. It processes synchronous code, then checks for completed async work. `setTimeout`, `fetch`, DOM events — all go through the event loop queue and run when the current synchronous code finishes.

use_when: Any time your code fetches data, reads files, waits for user input, or does anything that takes an unknown amount of time.

definition.prompt: Why does JavaScript use asynchronous code for operations like API calls?
options: Because it's faster than synchronous code
To prevent the browser from freezing while waiting for slow operations
Because APIs require it
Because synchronous code can't handle network requests
definition.explanation: JavaScript runs on a single thread. If it waited synchronously for an API response (which could take seconds), the entire page would freeze — no user interaction, no animations, nothing. Async lets slow work happen in the background.

definition.prompt: You fetch data and log it on the very next line, but get `undefined`. What's the most likely cause?
options: The API returned nothing
The data is the wrong type
The fetch is async — the data hasn't arrived yet when the log runs
You need to declare the variable first
definition.explanation: Async operations don't pause the code. The next line runs immediately — before the fetch completes. You need to `await` the result or use `.then()` to access the data only after it arrives.

definition.prompt: What does 'single-threaded' mean in JavaScript?
options: JavaScript can only run in one browser tab
JavaScript processes one thing at a time — no true parallel execution
JavaScript only supports one async operation at a time
JavaScript runs on a single core always
definition.explanation: Single-threaded means JavaScript has one call stack — it executes one operation at a time. Async doesn't create real parallelism; it lets slow work (network, timers) happen outside the JS thread and re-enter via the event loop when done.

name: Promises
what_it_is: A Promise is an object that represents the eventual result of an async operation — it's a placeholder for a value that will arrive in the future. A promise can be pending (waiting), fulfilled (succeeded with a value), or rejected (failed with an error). You chain `.then()` to handle success and `.catch()` to handle failure. Most modern web APIs return promises.
analogy: An order confirmation slip — you don't have the food yet, but you have a promise that it's coming. You can specify what to do when it arrives (.then) and what to do if it's cancelled (.catch).

examples.text: `fetch('https://api.example.com/data')` returns a Promise. `.then(res => res.json())` runs when the response arrives. `.catch(err => console.error(err))` runs if it fails. You chain them: fetch → then → catch.

`Promise.all([fetch(url1), fetch(url2)])` waits for both fetches to complete and gives you both results at once. If either fails, the whole thing rejects. Use it when you need multiple async results before continuing.

A common mistake: not returning the promise inside a `.then()`. If you have another async operation inside `.then()` and don't return it, the chain doesn't wait for it — the next `.then()` runs immediately with `undefined`.

use_when: When working with APIs, timers, or any operation that gives you a value in the future. If a function returns a Promise, you need .then()/.catch() or async/await to use its result.

definition.prompt: What is a JavaScript Promise?
options: A type of variable that stores async data
An object representing a value that will be available in the future
A function that runs after a delay
A guarantee that code will run without errors
definition.explanation: A Promise is a proxy for a future value. It starts pending, then either fulfills (with a value) or rejects (with an error). You attach handlers with `.then()` and `.catch()` to react when it settles.

definition.prompt: What method do you chain on a Promise to handle a successful result?
options: .catch()
.finally()
.then()
.resolve()
definition.explanation: `.then(callback)` runs when the promise fulfills. The callback receives the resolved value. You can chain multiple `.then()` calls — each one receives the return value of the previous.

definition.prompt: What does `Promise.all([p1, p2, p3])` do?
options: Runs the promises one after another
Returns the first promise to resolve
Waits for all promises and returns all results; rejects if any one fails
Runs all promises and ignores any failures
definition.explanation: `Promise.all` takes an array of promises and returns a new promise that resolves with an array of all results — but only when ALL of them have resolved. If any one rejects, the whole thing rejects immediately.

name: async / await
what_it_is: `async/await` is syntax that makes promises easier to read and write. Adding `async` before a function makes it return a promise automatically. Inside it, `await` pauses execution until a promise resolves, then gives you the value directly — so async code reads almost like synchronous code, top to bottom. It's the standard way to write async code in modern JavaScript.
analogy: Telling a story in plain sequence — 'I ordered food, I waited, I ate' — instead of 'I ordered food and set up a callback for when it arrives and another for if it doesn't.'

examples.text: `const data = await fetch(url).then(r => r.json())` — `await` pauses until the fetch and JSON parsing are done, then puts the result directly into `data`. No `.then()` chain needed for the rest of the logic.

To handle errors with async/await, wrap the `await` in try/catch: `try { const data = await fetchUser() } catch (err) { console.error(err) }`. This is the async/await equivalent of `.catch()` on a promise chain.

You can only use `await` inside an `async` function. If you try `await` at the top level of a regular script, you'll get a SyntaxError. Most modern runtimes and bundlers support top-level await in modules, but inside functions it's always safe.

use_when: Any time you're working with promises — calling APIs, reading files, querying databases. async/await makes the code linear and readable.

definition.prompt: What does `await` do inside an async function?
options: Cancels the promise if it takes too long
Pauses execution until the promise resolves, then returns the value
Converts a callback to a promise
Runs the next line immediately without waiting
definition.explanation: `await` suspends the async function at that point until the promise settles. If it resolves, you get the value. If it rejects, it throws (which try/catch can handle). Execution resumes from where it paused.

definition.prompt: What does adding `async` before a function declaration do?
options: Makes the function run in parallel with other code
Makes the function always return a Promise
Allows the function to take more arguments
Makes the function run faster
definition.explanation: An `async` function always returns a Promise. If you `return 42` from it, callers receive `Promise.resolve(42)`. This is what allows `await` to work inside it.

definition.prompt: Where can you use the `await` keyword?
options: Anywhere in any JavaScript file
Only inside functions marked with `async`
Only at the top level of a file
Only inside `.then()` callbacks
definition.explanation: `await` can only be used inside `async` functions (or at the module top level in modern environments). Using it outside an async function is a SyntaxError.

name: Import & Export
what_it_is: Modules are how code is split across multiple files. `export` marks something in one file as available to other files. `import` brings it in. This is how every modern JavaScript project is organized — instead of one giant file, logic is split into files that each do one thing, and they share what they need through imports and exports. This is called ES Modules (ESM).
analogy: A storefront (export) and a shopping cart (import) — each file offers what it has, and other files take only what they need.

examples.text: In `utils.js`: `export function formatDate(d) { ... }`. In another file: `import { formatDate } from './utils.js'`. Now `formatDate` is available to use. The `{}` syntax is for named exports.

Default exports: `export default function App() { ... }` — each file can have one default export. You import it without braces and can name it anything: `import App from './App.js'` or `import MyApp from './App.js'`.

`import React, { useState, useEffect } from 'react'` — importing the default export (`React`) and two named exports (`useState`, `useEffect`) from the same module in one line. This pattern is everywhere in React code.

use_when: Any time you split code across files — which is all the time in real projects. Import the things you need; export the things other files should be able to use.

definition.prompt: What does `export` do in a JavaScript module?
options: Sends data to an external API
Makes a value, function, or class available for other files to import
Copies the file to another location
Runs the code immediately when the file loads
definition.explanation: `export` marks something as part of the module's public interface. Other files can then `import` it. Without export, everything in a file is private to that file.

definition.prompt: What is the difference between a named export and a default export?
options: Named exports are faster; default exports are for large objects
A file can have many named exports but only one default export
Default exports use curly braces; named exports don't
Named exports are for functions; default exports are for data
definition.explanation: Named exports: `export function foo()` — imported with braces `{ foo }`. Default export: `export default foo` — one per file, imported without braces and can be named anything on import.

definition.prompt: You import `{ helper }` from a file but get `undefined`. What's the most likely cause?
options: The function name is too short
The file exports `helper` as a default export, not a named export
You can't import functions, only values
The file hasn't finished loading
definition.explanation: Named import `{ helper }` only works if the file has `export function helper` or `export const helper`. If it uses `export default helper`, you import it without braces: `import helper from './file'`.

name: CommonJS (require)
what_it_is: CommonJS is Node.js's original module system, using `require()` to import and `module.exports` to export. Before ES Modules (import/export) were widely supported, CommonJS was the standard for all Node.js code. You'll still encounter it in older codebases, most Node.js documentation, and anywhere that hasn't migrated to ESM. Knowing both lets you read and work in either style.
analogy: An older filing system — still widely used, works reliably, just a different syntax than the newer one. You need to know both to work across different projects.

examples.text: `const fs = require('fs')` — imports Node's built-in file system module. `const express = require('express')` — imports the Express framework. This is the CommonJS way to import.

`module.exports = { myFunction, myValue }` — exports an object from the file. Or `module.exports = myFunction` to export a single thing. The receiving end does `const { myFunction } = require('./myFile')`.

A file with `"type": "module"` in package.json uses ESM (`import/export`). Without it, Node.js defaults to CommonJS (`require`). This is why you sometimes get `SyntaxError: Cannot use import statement` — your environment expects `require`.

use_when: When working in Node.js without a bundler, reading older tutorials or packages, or when a project's package.json doesn't have `"type": "module"`.

definition.prompt: What is the CommonJS equivalent of ES Module's `import`?
options: load()
require()
include()
fetch()
definition.explanation: `require()` is the CommonJS function for importing modules. `const express = require('express')` is equivalent to `import express from 'express'` in ESM.

definition.prompt: How do you export a function in CommonJS?
options: export function myFn()
export default myFn
module.exports = myFn
exports myFn
definition.explanation: In CommonJS, you assign to `module.exports` to export. `module.exports = myFn` exports a single thing; `module.exports = { myFn, otherFn }` exports multiple.

definition.prompt: Why might you get `SyntaxError: Cannot use import statement in a module` in a Node.js project?
options: Your Node.js version is too old to support modules
The file is using ESM import/export but Node expects CommonJS require
You forgot to install the import package
import only works in the browser, not Node.js
definition.explanation: Node.js defaults to CommonJS. To use `import/export`, you need `"type": "module"` in package.json, or use the `.mjs` file extension. Without it, Node sees `import` and throws a SyntaxError.

name: Package Manager
what_it_is: Think of it like an app store for code. Instead of manually downloading libraries and figuring out where to put them, a package manager handles the whole install process — it fetches the code, puts it in the right folder, and keeps a record of what your project uses. In JavaScript projects, that tool is npm.
analogy: Like an app store: search, install, update, and remove code libraries without touching any files yourself.

examples.text: When you want to add a date-formatting library to your project, instead of finding it online and copying files manually, you run one command and it's installed and ready to use.
If a library you're using has a bug fix, your package manager can update it with a single command instead of you having to find and replace files yourself.
npm, pip (Python), cargo (Rust), and gem (Ruby) are all package managers for their respective languages.

use_when: Any time you want to add, update, or remove an external library from your project.

options: Installs, updates, and removes code libraries for your project
Compresses your code for deployment
Checks your code for bugs
Runs your tests automatically
definition.explanation: A package manager fetches code libraries from the internet, puts them in the right place, and tracks what your project depends on — so you don't have to manage any of that manually.
definition.prompt: What does a package manager do?

options: pip
cargo
npm
gem
definition.explanation: npm (Node Package Manager) comes bundled with Node.js and is the standard package manager for JavaScript projects.
definition.prompt: Which of these is JavaScript's built-in package manager?

options: Package managers are faster to type
They handle versioning, placement, and tracking automatically
Manual downloads give you more control
Package managers only work online
definition.explanation: Package managers automate the tedious parts: fetching the right version, putting files in the right folder, and recording what your project depends on so others can reproduce your setup.
definition.prompt: Why use a package manager instead of downloading libraries manually?

name: npm
what_it_is: npm stands for Node Package Manager. It's a command-line tool that comes installed with Node.js — you use it to install libraries, run scripts, and manage your project's dependencies. It also connects to a massive online registry of open-source packages at npmjs.com where nearly a million JavaScript libraries live.
analogy: Like the App Store combined with a download manager: search for something, install it, and npm handles everything.

examples.text: Running `npm install` in a project folder downloads all the libraries the project needs — like restoring a phone from backup, all your apps reappear.
Running `npm install lodash` adds the lodash library to your project and records it in package.json so anyone who clones your project can get it too.
`npm run build` triggers a build script you've defined — npm acts as a task runner, not just a package installer.

example_command:
npm install
npm install express
npm run dev
use_when: When you need to install packages, run project scripts, or manage dependencies in a Node.js project.

options: Node Package Manager
New Program Module
Node Project Maker
Network Package Monitor
definition.explanation: npm stands for Node Package Manager. It ships with Node.js and is the primary tool for managing JavaScript packages.
definition.prompt: What does npm stand for?

options: Creates a new project
Downloads all dependencies listed in package.json
Publishes your package online
Updates Node.js
definition.explanation: `npm install` reads your package.json, finds all listed dependencies, and downloads them into a node_modules folder — restoring everything the project needs to run.
definition.prompt: What does `npm install` do when run in a project folder?

options: Compile TypeScript only
Run scripts defined in package.json
Manage git commits
Edit HTML files
definition.explanation: npm doubles as a task runner. Any script you define in the `scripts` section of package.json can be run with `npm run <script-name>`.
definition.prompt: Besides installing packages, what else can npm do?

name: npx
what_it_is: npx lets you run a package without installing it first. It's like borrowing a tool from a library instead of buying it — npx downloads it temporarily, runs it, and then it's gone. It comes with npm and is especially useful for one-time commands like scaffolding a new project.
analogy: Like renting a tool for a single job rather than buying and storing it permanently.

examples.text: Running `npx create-react-app my-app` sets up a brand new React project without you needing to install create-react-app on your machine.
If you need to run a one-off command from a package you don't want cluttering your global installs, npx handles it and leaves no trace.
`npx prettier --write .` runs the Prettier formatter on your files without requiring a global install.

use_when: When you want to run a CLI tool once without installing it globally, or scaffold a new project.

options: Install packages permanently
Run packages without installing them first
Update npm to the latest version
Publish packages to npmjs.com
definition.explanation: npx runs a package temporarily — downloading it if needed, executing it, and leaving no permanent install behind. Great for one-off CLI tools.
definition.prompt: What is the main purpose of npx?

options: npx is faster at installing
npm install adds to package.json, npx runs without installing
npx only works on Mac
They do the same thing
definition.explanation: `npm install` adds a package to your project (or machine) permanently. `npx` runs a package once without permanently installing it anywhere.
definition.prompt: How does npx differ from npm install?

options: It's faster than npm
It always uses the latest version without a stale global install
It skips installing dependencies
create-react-app requires npx to work
definition.explanation: Using npx means you always get the latest version of create-react-app without needing to update a global install manually.
definition.prompt: Why is `npx create-react-app my-app` the recommended way to start a React project?

name: package.json
what_it_is: package.json is the project's ID card — a file at the root of every Node.js project that records the project's name, version, what libraries it depends on, and what commands you can run. Whenever you install a package with npm, it's recorded here automatically.
analogy: Like a recipe card: it lists all the ingredients (dependencies) your project needs and instructions (scripts) for how to run it.

examples.text: When you share your project with a teammate, they run `npm install` and npm reads package.json to know exactly which libraries to download.
The `scripts` section lets you define shortcuts — `npm run dev` might start your local server, saving you from typing a long command every time.
The `main` field tells Node.js which file is the entry point when someone requires your package.

use_when: Always present in Node.js projects. You read it to understand dependencies; npm writes to it automatically when you install packages.

options: Store your source code
Record the project's dependencies, scripts, and metadata
Configure your text editor
List all the files in the project
definition.explanation: package.json is the central config file for a Node.js project — it tracks which libraries the project needs, what version it is, and what commands are available to run.
definition.prompt: What is the main purpose of package.json?

options: It deletes existing dependencies
It downloads all listed dependencies into node_modules
It publishes your package
It runs your tests
definition.explanation: npm reads the dependencies listed in package.json and downloads them all into a node_modules folder, making the project ready to run.
definition.prompt: What happens when you run `npm install` with a package.json present?

options: Write JavaScript directly in JSON
Define shortcut commands you can run with npm run
List all your project's files
Set environment variables
definition.explanation: Scripts are named shortcuts — `"dev": "node server.js"` means you can type `npm run dev` instead of the full command, making common tasks easier to remember.
definition.prompt: What does the `scripts` section of package.json let you do?

name: node_modules
what_it_is: node_modules is the folder where npm puts all the code it downloads when you install packages. It can get huge — hundreds of megabytes — because each package you install may depend on other packages, which depend on more packages. You never edit this folder directly, and you never commit it to git.
analogy: Like a storage room behind a restaurant kitchen: all the supplies are there but you never go in yourself — the chefs (npm) manage it.

examples.text: After running `npm install`, a node_modules folder appears containing all your project's dependencies. If you delete it, just run `npm install` again to restore everything.
node_modules is listed in .gitignore because it can be hundreds of megabytes — instead, you commit package.json and teammates regenerate it with `npm install`.
Each package in node_modules may contain its own node_modules, which is why a project with 10 direct dependencies may have 500+ packages installed.

use_when: It exists automatically after npm install. Never edit it directly; never commit it.

options: Where you write your app's source code
Where npm stores downloaded package code
A configuration file for Node.js
A folder you create manually
definition.explanation: node_modules is automatically created by npm to store all the library code your project depends on. You never touch it directly.
definition.prompt: What is the node_modules folder?

options: Git can't handle folders
It can be huge and is fully regenerated by npm install
It contains secrets
It's a system folder
definition.explanation: node_modules can be hundreds of megabytes and is easily recreated by running `npm install`. Committing it wastes space and causes merge conflicts.
definition.prompt: Why is node_modules excluded from git commits?

options: Reinstall Node.js
Run npm install
Restore it from git
Rewrite your package.json
definition.explanation: npm install reads package.json and re-downloads everything. node_modules is fully disposable — it's meant to be regenerated, not preserved.
definition.prompt: What should you do if you accidentally delete your node_modules folder?

name: npm Scripts
what_it_is: npm Scripts are named commands you define in the `scripts` section of package.json. Think of them as keyboard shortcuts for your project — instead of typing a long command like `node --watch src/index.js`, you define it once as `"dev"` and just run `npm run dev` from then on.
analogy: Like saved contacts on your phone — instead of typing a full number, you just tap a name.

examples.text: Defining `"start": "node index.js"` means anyone who clones your project can run it with `npm start` without knowing the internals.
A `"build"` script might run a bundler, and a `"test"` script might run your test suite — teammates don't need to know the exact commands, just `npm run build` and `npm test`.
`"lint": "eslint . --fix"` can be hooked into pre-commit workflows so code is auto-formatted before every git commit.

example_command:
npm run dev
npm start
npm test
npm run build
use_when: Any time you want to create a shortcut for a command that your project or teammates run frequently.

options: In a scripts.js file
In the scripts section of package.json
In node_modules
In .env
definition.explanation: npm scripts live in the `scripts` field of package.json. Each key is the script name and the value is the shell command to run.
definition.prompt: Where do you define npm scripts?

options: node dev
run dev
npm run dev
npm dev start
definition.explanation: `npm run <script-name>` is how you execute any custom script. The two built-in shortcuts are `npm start` and `npm test`, which don't need the `run` keyword.
definition.prompt: How do you run a script named 'dev'?

options: Scripts run faster than direct commands
They standardize commands so anyone can run the project the same way
npm requires you to use scripts
Direct commands don't work in Node.js
definition.explanation: Scripts create a shared interface for your project. A new teammate doesn't need to learn your exact build command — they run `npm run build` and it works.
definition.prompt: Why define scripts in package.json instead of just running commands directly?

name: .env Files
what_it_is: A .env file is where you store secret or environment-specific values — things like API keys, database passwords, or port numbers — outside of your code. Your app reads these values at runtime, so you can change them without touching the source code. The file is always in .gitignore so secrets never end up on GitHub.
analogy: Like a locked sticky note next to your computer: your code knows to look there, but it never gets shared or committed.

examples.text: Instead of writing your API key directly in your code (which would expose it on GitHub), you put `OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...` in .env and access it as `process.env.OPENAI_API_KEY` in code.
You can have a `.env.development` with a local database URL and a `.env.production` with the live database URL — same code, different behavior per environment.
The dotenv npm package loads .env values into `process.env` at startup with one line: `require('dotenv').config()`.

use_when: Whenever your app needs secret keys, connection strings, or any value that differs between environments (local, staging, production).

options: Store JavaScript functions
Store secret or environment-specific values outside your code
Configure git
List installed packages
definition.explanation: .env files keep sensitive values (API keys, passwords) and environment-specific config out of your source code, where they'd be visible to anyone who can see the codebase.
definition.prompt: What is the main purpose of a .env file?

options: Git doesn't support .env files
They contain secrets that would be exposed publicly
They're too large for git
They change every time you run the app
definition.explanation: .env files often contain API keys and passwords. Committing them to a public (or even private) repo risks exposing credentials — they belong in .gitignore.
definition.prompt: Why should .env files never be committed to git?

options: import .env
read('.env')
process.env.VARIABLE_NAME
env.get('VARIABLE_NAME')
definition.explanation: After loading dotenv, environment variables become available on the global `process.env` object. `process.env.MY_KEY` reads the value of `MY_KEY` from your .env file.
definition.prompt: How do you access a value from .env inside Node.js code?

name: dependencies vs devDependencies
