definition.explanation: Hallucination is when a model generates plausible-sounding but incorrect or fabricated information — stated with the same confidence as accurate responses.
definition.prompt: What is hallucination in the context of LLMs?

options: They are programmed to lie sometimes
They generate statistically likely text, not verified facts — they don't distinguish true from false
They have too little training data
Hallucinations only happen at high temperature
definition.explanation: LLMs don't have a concept of truth — they predict likely next tokens. When they don't 'know' something, they generate what sounds plausible, which can be completely wrong.
definition.prompt: Why do LLMs hallucinate?

options: Trust them — LLMs are trained on accurate data
Verify important facts from authoritative sources before relying on them
Only trust claims about code
Ask the LLM to confirm its answer
definition.explanation: LLMs can be wrong confidently. For anything important — medical, legal, technical facts — verify independently. Asking the model 'are you sure?' doesn't reliably detect hallucinations.
definition.prompt: How should you handle factual claims from an LLM?

name: Prompts & System Prompts
what_it_is: A prompt is what you send to an LLM — your question or instruction. A system prompt is a special instruction that sets the model's behavior and persona before any conversation starts. You can't see the system prompt in chat interfaces, but it's what makes Claude behave like Claude Code vs. the default Claude — it pre-loads context and instructions.
analogy: The user prompt is what you say to a chef. The system prompt is the briefing the chef got before you arrived — their instructions, specialties, and what they're allowed to serve.

examples.text: System prompt: 'You are a helpful customer support agent for Acme Corp. Only answer questions about our products. Always be polite.' This shapes every response before the user says anything.
When building an AI feature in your app, you write the system prompt in your code and send user messages as the conversation. Users see responses but never the system prompt.
Prompt engineering is the practice of crafting prompts carefully — being specific, providing examples, setting format instructions — to get consistently good outputs.

use_when: When building AI features: the system prompt defines behavior. When using AI tools: understanding prompts helps you get better results.

options: The terminal command to start an AI model
Instructions sent to the model before the conversation that set its persona and behavior
A prompt the system generates automatically
The model's response to your first message
definition.explanation: A system prompt is developer-controlled context — it pre-loads the model with role, instructions, and constraints before the user interaction begins.
definition.prompt: What is a system prompt?

options: The user, before each session
The developer, in the application code
The AI model itself
OpenAI or Anthropic
definition.explanation: The developer writes the system prompt in the app's code. It defines how the AI behaves for that product — what it knows, how it responds, what it won't do.
definition.prompt: When building an AI-powered app, who typically writes the system prompt?

options: Writing code that generates prompts automatically
The practice of crafting effective prompts to get reliable, high-quality outputs
Engineering a new AI model
Optimizing prompts for speed
definition.explanation: Prompt engineering is the skill of writing prompts that reliably produce good outputs — being specific, providing examples, structuring instructions clearly, and testing iteratively.
definition.prompt: What is prompt engineering?

name: Models & Costs
what_it_is: LLM providers offer multiple model tiers — more capable models cost more per token; smaller, faster models cost less. Choosing the right model for a task matters: using the most powerful model for everything is expensive; using a weak model for complex reasoning gives bad results. Most providers charge separately for input tokens (what you send) and output tokens (what the model generates).
analogy: Like choosing between economy, business, and first class — higher tiers cost more but offer more capability. Matching the tier to the task saves money.

examples.text: Claude Haiku is fast and cheap — great for simple classification, summaries, or high-volume tasks. Claude Opus is slower and expensive — worth it for complex reasoning or nuanced writing.
Output tokens typically cost more than input tokens — generating 1000 words costs more than sending 1000 words. This matters when designing prompts for production apps.
When building AI features, architects often route simple queries to cheap models and complex ones to powerful models — maximizing quality per dollar.

use_when: When choosing which model to use in an API call — balance the task complexity against cost to avoid over-spending or under-performing.

options: Different models support different languages
Tasks vary in complexity — simpler models are cheaper for simpler tasks
Smaller models are always better
Multiple tiers are a legal requirement
definition.explanation: Not every task needs the most powerful model. Providers offer tiers so you can pay for capability proportional to the task — simple tasks use cheap models, complex tasks use powerful ones.
definition.prompt: Why do AI providers offer multiple model tiers?

options: Per API call, regardless of size
Per token — separately for input (what you send) and output (what the model generates)
Per second of processing time
A flat monthly subscription only
definition.explanation: LLM APIs charge per token — typically with separate rates for input and output. Output (generation) tokens often cost more than input (prompt) tokens.
definition.prompt: How are costs typically calculated for LLM API calls?

options: When the task requires deep reasoning
For high-volume, simple tasks where cost matters more than maximum capability
For safety-critical applications
When you want the fastest cold start
definition.explanation: For simple tasks (classification, short summaries, high volume) a smaller model is often sufficient and significantly cheaper. Save the powerful models for tasks that actually need them.
definition.prompt: When would you choose a smaller, cheaper model over the most powerful one?

name: Embeddings
what_it_is: An embedding is a way of representing text as a list of numbers (a vector) that captures its meaning. Texts with similar meaning end up with similar numbers — so you can mathematically compare how related two pieces of text are. Embeddings power semantic search (find me things *similar* to this), recommendation systems, and RAG.
analogy: Like plotting cities on a map — similar cities cluster together. Embeddings put text on a 'meaning map' where similar texts live close together.

examples.text: 'Dog' and 'puppy' have very similar embeddings because they mean similar things. 'Dog' and 'spaceship' have very different embeddings.
You embed all your documents and store the vectors. When a user searches, you embed their query and find the stored documents with the most similar vectors — semantic search.
OpenAI's `text-embedding-3-small` model generates embeddings via API. You send text, you get back a vector of 1536 numbers you can store and compare.

use_when: When building semantic search, recommendation features, or anything that needs to find 'similar' content rather than exact keyword matches.

options: Text that's been compressed
A numerical vector representing the meaning of text, where similar meanings produce similar vectors
A way to encrypt text
A token count for a piece of text
definition.explanation: An embedding converts text into a list of numbers (a vector) in a way that captures semantic meaning. Similar texts produce similar vectors, enabling mathematical comparison of meaning.
definition.prompt: What is a text embedding?

options: Search faster
Find semantically similar content even when exact words don't match
Search across languages
Find exact phrase matches
definition.explanation: Keyword search matches exact words. Semantic search with embeddings finds conceptually related content — 'canine companion' and 'dog owner tips' are similar even with zero shared keywords.
definition.prompt: What can you do with embeddings that you can't do with keyword search?

options: As a replacement for databases
Generated and stored for a dataset, then compared to a query embedding to find the most similar items
Sent directly to an LLM as input
As a way to compress images
definition.explanation: The typical pattern: embed your documents and store the vectors. At query time, embed the search query and find stored vectors with the highest cosine similarity — those are the most relevant results.
definition.prompt: How are embeddings typically used in practice?

name: RAG
what_it_is: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) is a pattern for grounding an LLM's answers in specific documents. Instead of relying on the model's training data, you retrieve relevant documents first (using embeddings or search), then pass them to the model as context along with the user's question. The model answers based on what you retrieved — reducing hallucination and enabling answers about private or recent data.
analogy: Like giving an open-book exam instead of a closed one — the model uses the retrieved documents to answer, not just memory.

examples.text: A company builds a customer support bot. They embed all their documentation. When a user asks a question, the app retrieves the most relevant docs and passes them to the LLM: 'Answer based on these docs: [...]'.
RAG lets you query your own data — company wikis, private docs, recent news — that the model was never trained on. The retrieval step bridges the gap.
The pipeline: user question → embed question → vector search → retrieve top N docs → build prompt with docs + question → send to LLM → return grounded answer.

use_when: When building AI features that need to answer questions about specific documents, private data, or information outside the model's training data.

options: A type of neural network architecture
A pattern that retrieves relevant documents and passes them to an LLM as context before generating a response
A way to fine-tune a model
A token compression technique
definition.explanation: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) combines document retrieval with LLM generation — fetch relevant context first, then have the model answer using that context, not just its training.
definition.prompt: What is RAG?

options: It uses a lower temperature
The model answers from retrieved documents rather than relying on potentially faulty training memory
RAG uses more powerful models
It verifies answers against a database
definition.explanation: When you give the model the actual relevant documents in the prompt, it can answer from that grounded context rather than 'remembering' from training — reducing invented facts.
definition.prompt: Why does RAG reduce hallucination compared to a plain LLM call?

options: Fine-tuning the model
Generating the response
Retrieving relevant documents using search or embeddings
Setting the temperature
definition.explanation: RAG always retrieves first. The user question is used to find the most relevant documents (via vector search, keyword search, or both), which are then passed to the LLM as context.
definition.prompt: What step comes before calling the LLM in a RAG pipeline?

name: Using AI APIs in Code
what_it_is: AI APIs like Claude or OpenAI let you add AI capabilities to your app with a few lines of code. You send a prompt (and optionally a system prompt) via HTTP, and you get a generated response back. The APIs are standard REST — you authenticate with an API key, send JSON, get JSON back.
analogy: Like calling any other API — you send a request with your message, you get a response with the AI's reply. The complexity is in the prompt, not the API call.

examples.text: Using the Anthropic SDK: `const msg = await anthropic.messages.create({ model: 'claude-opus-4-6', max_tokens: 1024, messages: [{ role: 'user', content: 'Hello' }] })` — that's a complete API call.
The API is stateless — each call is independent. To maintain a conversation, you send the full message history in every request.
Streaming lets the model send tokens as they're generated rather than waiting for the full response — better UX for long outputs, looks like the AI is 'typing'.

use_when: When adding AI features to your app — summarization, classification, generation, question-answering — without building your own model.

options: A special server setup
An API key and a standard HTTP request with your prompt as JSON
Training your own model first
A GPU on your machine
definition.explanation: AI APIs are standard REST APIs — you authenticate with an API key and send JSON with your messages. The provider handles the model; you just make the call.
definition.prompt: What do you need to call an AI API like Claude or OpenAI?

options: The API requires it for billing
AI APIs are stateless — they have no memory between calls, so you provide conversation context each time
For security reasons
To help the model understand the language
definition.explanation: AI APIs are stateless — each call is independent. To maintain a conversation, you include all previous messages in the `messages` array on every request.
definition.prompt: Why do you need to send the full message history in each API call?

options: Sending audio to the model
Receiving tokens progressively as they're generated rather than waiting for the complete response
Streaming video with AI subtitles
Sending large files to the API
definition.explanation: Streaming sends tokens to your app as they're generated, so you can display partial responses immediately — creating the 'typing' effect users see in chat interfaces.
definition.prompt: What is streaming in the context of AI API responses?

name: AI Agents
what_it_is: An AI agent is a system where an LLM can take actions — not just generate text, but use tools like web search, code execution, or API calls to complete a task. The model reasons about what to do, calls a tool, gets a result, reasons about the result, and repeats until the task is done. Claude Code is an agent: it reads files, writes code, and runs commands — not just chat.
analogy: Like giving a very smart assistant a computer and a to-do list — instead of just telling you what to do, they actually do it.

examples.text: A research agent is given 'summarize the latest AI news'. It calls a search tool, gets results, reads the articles, and writes a summary — all without human steps in between.
Agents are built with a loop: LLM → decide to use tool → call tool → observe result → LLM again → repeat until done. The loop is called 'ReAct' (Reason + Act).
The challenge with agents is reliability — each tool call is a chance to fail or hallucinate. Good agent design adds error handling, retry logic, and human oversight checkpoints.

use_when: When building AI systems that need to take multi-step actions — not just generate text but do research, write code, query APIs, or manage files autonomously.

options: Agents use more powerful models
Agents can take actions (use tools, make API calls) to complete tasks, not just generate text
Agents are faster
Agents require no prompting
definition.explanation: A simple LLM call generates text. An agent is a system that can reason, decide to use tools, execute them, observe the results, and loop — completing tasks that require multiple steps.
definition.prompt: What distinguishes an AI agent from a simple LLM call?

options: A model fine-tuned for a specific task
A function the agent can call to interact with the world — search, code execution, API calls, file access
A prompt template
A type of embedding
definition.explanation: Tools give agents capabilities beyond text generation. Common tools: web search, code interpreters, database queries, API calls, file reads. The agent decides which tool to use and when.
definition.prompt: What is a 'tool' in the context of AI agents?

options: They cost too much to run
They are unreliable — each action step can fail, hallucinate, or produce unexpected results
They only work with certain models
They require special hardware
definition.explanation: Agents chain multiple LLM calls and tool uses together. Errors compound — a hallucinated intermediate step can derail the whole task. Robust agents need validation, retries, and human oversight.
definition.prompt: What is the main challenge with autonomous AI agents?
